text:
EDMUND. I know not why, save that he covets gold.
He brands me base, a cuckoo in the nest.
But whether I was strictly bred or no,
That burden rests upon my mother’s soul.
Yet that I am as stoutly framed, my lord—
Blessings upon the blood that fashioned me!—
Survey our statures and decide the truth.
If ailing Lord Roland sired us both,
And were our sire, and this boy takes his shape,
O frail Lord Roland, father, on my knees
I praise the fates I was not stamped like thee!
DUKE VINCENT. What brazen spirit hath the heavens sent?
DUCHESS CLARA. He wears the very cast of Count Valerius;
The bold demeanor of his eye matches.
Do you not trace some shadow of that lord
In the broad shoulders of this gentleman?
DUKE VINCENT. My sight hath measured his proportions well,
And finds them absolute Valerius. Boy, declare,
What drives thee to demand thy brother’s wealth?
EDMUND. Because he bears a hollow frame, like Roland.
With half my girth he claims the whole estate:
A brittle reed commands ten thousand crowns!
ARTHUR. My sovereign lord, when my late father lived,
The Count Valerius kept my father close—
EDMUND. Soft, sir, this argument wins not the gold.
Your tale must prove how close he kept my mother.
ARTHUR. And once dispatched him to the northern front,
To Scottish borders, there to hold the line
And parley with the rebels of the march.
The Count took profit of his distant post,
And in the interim lodged at our estate;
How he conquered there I blush to tell;
But facts remain: vast tracts of hills and streams
Between my father and my mother lay,
As I have heard my father swear himself,
When this robust adventurer was conceived.
Upon his dying bed he drafted terms
To leave his wealth to me, and swore an oath
That this my mother’s child was none of his;
And if he were, he tumbled to the earth
Some sixteen weeks before the natural term.
Therefore, my liege, grant me my rightful due,
My father’s wealth, as was my father’s will.
DUKE VINCENT. Sirrah, your brother is a lawful child;
Your father’s lady bore him within bonds,
And if she strayed, the trespass was her own;
A peril native to the common lot
Of wedded men. Tell me, if this great Count,
Who, as you claim, took pains to forge this youth,
Had of your father sought to take the boy?
In truth, good youth, your father might have held
This colt, dropped in his barn, from all the earth;
Indeed, he might; thus, if he were the Count’s,
The Count could not compel him; nor your sire,
Though none of his, discard him. Thus it stands:
Your mother’s son did prove your father’s heir;
Your father’s heir must hold your father’s gold.
ARTHUR. Shall then my father’s testament be void
To cast aside a rogue that shares no blood?
EDMUND. Of no more force to cast me out, good sir,
Than was his frail intent to sire me, I think.
DUCHESS CLARA. Which path wouldst thou prefer: to be a Roland,
And like thy brother, gorge upon the wealth,
Or the acknowledged blood of brave Valerius,
Master of nought but thine own consequence?
EDMUND. Madam, if my dear brother wore my flesh
And I wore his, Lord Roland’s heir, like him;
If my firm legs were merely walking-sticks,
My arms like withered vines, my cheek so pale
That I would fear to wear a heavy helm
Lest men should cry “The wind will snap him double!”
And, to his form, were heir to all this gold,
May I be rooted to this marble floor,
I’d give the coin to keep this rugged face.
I would not be Lord Twig for all the world.
DUCHESS CLARA. Thy spirit pleases me. Wilt leave thy gold,
Resign the manor to him, and ride with me?
I march for Spain and need a captain’s sword.
EDMUND. Brother, digest the gold, I’ll carve my fate.
Your brittle bones have bought ten thousand crowns,
Though auction off your frame, you’d fetch a groat.
Madam, I am your servant to the grave.
DUCHESS CLARA. Come then, and walk before me to the court.
EDMUND. A soldier’s grace yields up the path to rank.
DUKE VINCENT. What name dost bear?
EDMUND. Edmund, my sovereign, thus my name begins;
Edmund, the lady Roland’s eldest son.

EDMUND. Greed, mostly. Why else brand his own mother a whore?
He calls me base-born—a cuckoo slipped into the good Lord's nest.
Whether I was struck in the holy mint of marriage, I can't say; that’s a question for my mother's conscience, not mine.
But as for the actual making of me—well! Bless the rogue that put his back into it.
Look at us, my lord. Stand us side by side.
If sickly old Lord Roland really sired us both, and this boy is the very map of his father...
Then on my knees, I thank the stars I am a bastard!
DUKE VINCENT. The heavens have sent us a madman.
DUCHESS CLARA. No, Vincent, look closer. He has the very cut of Count Valerius.
The eye, the insolent jaw.
Do you not see my old friend in the sheer, massive bulk of him?
DUKE VINCENT. The mold is absolute Valerius, true enough.
Boy, speak up. What brings you to dispute your brother’s land?
EDMUND. Dispute? I only point out his half-measure of a body!
He'd take my whole estate with half a chest. Ten thousand crowns a year for a brittle reed!
ARTHUR. My sovereign lord, hear me. When my father lived—and the Count Valerius was ever at his side—
EDMUND. Careful, little brother. That’s no way to win an estate.
Tell the Duke how close he kept our mother.
ARTHUR. My father was dispatched to the Scottish borders. To parley, to hold the line. He was gone for months.
And the Count? The Count took his ease at our manor.
I blush to speak of what happened there, but truth is truth. Miles of harsh country and freezing rivers lay between my father’s bed and my mother.
My father said as much himself, realizing how this... giant came to be.
On his deathbed, he struck him from the will. He swore this child was no son of his! And even if he was, he dropped into the world sixteen weeks before the natural term.
Give me my land, my liege. It was my father's dying wish.
DUKE VINCENT. Now, hold. Your brother was born within the bonds of wedlock.
If your mother played false, the sin is hers—a hazard all husbands risk when they take a wife.
Look at it legally, boy. If the great Count had demanded this youth from your father, could he have him? No.
Your father could have locked up this calf, born in his own barn, from the whole world.
The Count couldn't claim him. And your father, despite his suspicions, couldn't simply cast him out.
The law is the law. Your mother's son is your father's heir, and the heir takes the land.
ARTHUR. So his dying breath means nothing? A sworn testament casting out a stranger, totally void?
EDMUND. Just as void as his attempt to sire me, Arthur.
DUCHESS CLARA. A question for you, giant.
Which is it to be? A Roland, stuffed with land and gold, much like your brother here?
Or the acknowledged bastard of Valerius, with nothing to your name but your own swagger?
EDMUND. Madam... if I had his shape.
If my legs were a pair of riding-crops, and my arms withered vines. If my face were so pinched I'd be afraid to wear a heavy helm, lest a stiff wind snap my neck in two!
And if, attached to that miserable frame, I had the ten thousand crowns...
I’d sell every acre just to buy this face back.
I wouldn't be Lord Twig for all the gold in Spain.
DUCHESS CLARA. I like you. Keep your face, leave the dirt to him, and ride with me.
I march for Spain, and I could use a captain's sword.
EDMUND. Choke on the gold, brother; I’ll take my chances in the field.
Your sickly face just bought you ten thousand crowns a year—though at a market, it wouldn't fetch a copper.
Madam, I am yours to the grave.
DUCHESS CLARA. Then lead the way to the court.
EDMUND. A soldier knows his place. After you.
DUKE VINCENT. What is your name, then?
EDMUND. Edmund, my lord. Just Edmund.
The Lady Roland’s eldest boy.

KING CHARLES. I am a sovereign, and I will be heard.
MORTIMER. My lord, the grievances that brought us armed
Cannot be mended by a monarch’s breath;
Therefore be mute.
PERCY. Then, executioner, make bare thy blade.
By all the saints that watch the turning world,
I swear Lord Mortimer is brave in words alone.
FREDERICK. Tell me, King Charles, wilt thou resign my right?
A thousand men stand fasting on this plain
Who shall not sup until they see thee fall.
SALISBURY. Deny us, and their slaughter is thine own;
For Frederick wears the armor of his truth.
PRINCE ARTHUR. If treason be the truth that Salisbury boasts,
Then hell is heaven, and every sin is pure.
PERCY. Whoever sired thee, boy, thy mother speaks;
For well I know that shrill and biting tone.
QUEEN ELEANOR. But thou art shaped like nothing natural,
A crooked shadow, nature's foul mistake,
Branded by God to warn the honest man,
Like venomous vipers or the rabid hound.
PERCY. O brass of Spain, masked in our English gold!
Whose father claims the dukedom of a patch,
As if a puddle should be called the sea.
Hast thou no shame, remembering thy descent,
To bare the malice of thy beggar’s heart?
FREDERICK. A beggar's rags were worth a kingly ransom
To teach this arrogant harpy what she is.
False Cressida was truer far than thou,
Though thy weak husband play the cuckold's part.
Never was Troy so ruined by a face
As this whole kingdom has been wrecked by thee.
His father marched triumphant through the East,
And made the foreign emperors bow their heads;
And had he wed a woman of his rank,
He might have left a peaceful realm to us.
But when he took this viper to his breast,
And gave thy ruined sire a golden seat,
That very hour he bred the bloody storm
That stripped his father's conquests from our hands
And seeded dark rebellion in our streets.
What caused this march of iron but thy pride?
Hadst thou been humble, we had never stirred;
And out of pity for this feeble king,
We would have let our title sleep in peace.
MORTIMER. But when we saw thy winter blight our spring,
And thy dark shadow choke our ripening fields,
We brought the hatchet to thy poisoned root.
And though the falling branches strike ourselves,
Know this: since we have raised our hands to strike,
We will not sheathe our weapons till thou fall,
Or water the dry earth with noble blood.
FREDERICK. In this resolve, I fling my challenge down;
I will not waste another breath on thee,
Since thou forbidd'st the wretched King to speak.
Sound out the horns! Let bloody banners fly;
We win the crown, or on this field we die!
QUEEN ELEANOR. Hold, Frederick.
FREDERICK. No, brawling queen, our talking time is done.
Thy bitter tongue hath cost ten thousand lives.
[Exeunt.]
SCENE III. A field of battle near Ravenspur
Alarums. Excursions. Enter Salisbury.
SALISBURY. Out of all breath, like hounds that lose the scent,
I lean upon my sword a little while;
For heavy blows received, and heavy dealt,
Have drained the marrow from my shaking limbs.
Despite my will, I must draw breath or die.
Enter Frederick, running.
FREDERICK. Weep, gentle skies, or strike me, cruel earth!
The day is lost, and Frederick's star falls down.
SALISBURY. What news, my lord? Is there no ray of hope?
Enter Percy.
PERCY. Our hope is dust, our fortune is a grave;
The lines are shattered, and the vanguard breaks.
What is your counsel? Whither do we run?
FREDERICK. Flight is in vain; their horses run like wind.
We are too spent to outpace their revenge.
Enter Mortimer.
MORTIMER. Ah, Salisbury, why didst thou leave the flank?
The thirsty soil has drunk thy nephew's blood,
Pierced by the heavy spike of Arthur's spear.
And in the bitter agony of death
He cried aloud, ringing across the din:
“Salisbury, avenge! Uncle, avenge my fall!”
Then, crushed beneath the trampling of the hooves,
That dyed their iron shoes in his red life,
The valiant youth surrendered up his soul.
SALISBURY. Then let the meadows gorge upon our blood!
I will unhorse myself, I will not flee.
Why stand we weeping like a frightened child,
Mourning our ruin while the butchers laugh,
And staring merely, as if all this woe
Were but a pageant played upon a stage?
Here on the bloodied grass I swear to God,
I will not take a step, nor yield an inch,
Until the dark night seals my heavy eyes,
Or Heaven grants me vengeance on my foes.

KING CHARLES. I am the King. I have the right to speak.
MORTIMER. My liege, the wounds that brought us to this field
Cannot be stitched with words.
Therefore, be mute.
PERCY. Draw your blade, executioner.
By God and all His saints, I swear
Mortimer’s only courage is in his throat.
FREDERICK. Well, Charles? Will you yield my right or no?
A thousand men stand fasting on this heath;
They will not break their fast until you fall.
SALISBURY. Deny us, and their blood is on your head.
Frederick fights in the armor of his truth.
PRINCE ARTHUR. If treason is the "truth" that Salisbury wears,
Then hell is heaven. Every sin is holy.
PERCY. Whoever sired you, boy, that is your mother speaking.
I know that shrill, complaining tune too well.
QUEEN ELEANOR. And you? You are a crooked, unnatural thing.
A foul mistake.
God marked you out so honest men would know to step aside,
As they would from a rabid dog or a viper in the grass.
PERCY. Spanish brass, painted with English gold!
Your father claims the dukedom of a mud-patch,
As if a gutter could be called the sea.
Have you no shame? Knowing the dirt you sprang from,
Why do you bare your beggar's heart to us?
FREDERICK. A beggar's rags would cost a kingly ransom
To teach this harpy her true worth.
False Cressida was a truer wife than you,
Though your poor husband plays the willing cuckold.
Troy itself was never wrecked so thoroughly
As this whole kingdom has been ruined by you!
His father marched his armies through the East.
He made the foreign emperors bow their heads.
Had he married a woman of his own rank,
He might have left a peaceful realm behind.
Instead, he took a viper to his bed.
He gave your ruined father a golden seat—and in that hour,
He brewed the very storm that washed his conquests away.
What brought this march of iron to your door? Your pride.
Had you been humble, we would have never stirred.
Out of pure pity for this feeble king,
We would have let our title sleep.
MORTIMER. But when we saw your winter kill our spring...
When your dark shadow choked our ripening fields...
We brought the hatchet to your poisoned root.
And though the falling branches hit our own heads,
We will not sheathe our swords. Not now.
We will hew you down, or drown the dry earth with our blood!
FREDERICK. I will not waste another breath on you,
Since you forbid the wretched King to speak.
Sound the horns. Let the bloody banners fly.
We win the crown today, or else we die.
QUEEN ELEANOR. Hold, Frederick—
FREDERICK. No! No more talking, brawling woman.
Your bitter tongue has cost ten thousand lives.
[Exeunt.]
SCENE III. A field of battle near Ravenspur
Alarums. Excursions. Enter Salisbury.
SALISBURY. I am spent.
Like a hound that’s lost the scent and run itself to near death,
I must lean on my sword. Just for a moment.
The blows I've taken—and the heavy ones I've dealt—
Have sucked the marrow straight from my bones.
I must draw breath or die where I stand.
Enter Frederick, running.
FREDERICK. Weep, heaven, or strike me down, earth!
The day is lost. My star is falling.
SALISBURY. What news, my lord? Is there no hope left?
Enter Percy.
PERCY. Hope is dirt. Our fortune is a mass grave.
The lines are shattered and the vanguard is broken.
What do we do? Where do we run?
FREDERICK. We don't. Their horses run like the wind;
We are too weak to outpace them.
Enter Mortimer.
MORTIMER. Salisbury! Why did you abandon the flank?
The soil has drunk your nephew's blood.
Arthur's heavy spear went right through him.
In the bitter agony of his last breath, he screamed over the din:
"Salisbury, avenge me! Uncle, avenge my fall!"
Then the horses took him.
Crushed beneath the hooves, their iron shoes dyed in his red life...
The boy gave up his soul.
SALISBURY. Then let the meadows choke on our blood!
I will kill my own horse. I will not flee.
Why are we weeping like frightened children?
Mourning our ruin while the butchers laugh at us—
Staring at the mud as if this were just a pageant on a stage!
Here. On this bloodied grass. I swear to God:
I will not take one step back. I will not yield an inch.
Not until the night seals my eyes shut,
Or Heaven gives me the vengeance I am owed.

VALERIUS. Why, didst thou not descend from the high clouds?
PEASANT. The clouds? By my faith, sir, I have never been so high. God save me from climbing to the clouds before my time. I am taking these capons to the lord justice, to settle a bloody fray between my cousin and the king's tax-gatherer.
CASSIUS. Why, brother, this is the perfect vessel for your suit. Let him present the capons to the King.
VALERIUS. Tell me, friend, can you offer a petition to the King with proper reverence?
PEASANT. Truly, sir, I have never petitioned any man but the tavern-keeper.
VALERIUS. Sirrah, step closer. Make no delay,
But give thy plump fowl to his Majesty.
Through me thou shalt find favor at his court.
Take this; here is good copper for thy pains.
Give me the wax and seal. Sirrah, canst thou
With a bold front deliver up my plea?
PEASANT. Aye, my lord.
VALERIUS. Then hold this scroll. And when you come to him, kneel upon the stones, kiss his signet ring, yield up the birds, and wait for your great reward. I will be near; do it without fear.
PEASANT. Leave it to me, sir; I warrant you.
VALERIUS. Sirrah, hast thou a gauntlet? Let me see it.
Here, Cassius, bind it up within the scroll,
For thou hast penned it like a humble slave.
And when thou hast presented it to the King,
Find me at the tavern and report his words.
PEASANT. God keep you, sir; I shall. [Exit.]
VALERIUS. Come, Cassius, let us walk. Lucius, attend me. [Exeunt.]
SCENE IV. The Royal Court.
Enter King Leontes, Queen Isabella, her sons Ferdinand and Roderigo, with Attendants. The King holds torn placards in his fist.
LEONTES. Look here, my lords, what insolence is this!
Was ever any sovereign of this realm
So openly abused, confronted thus,
And for the simple execution of the law,
Held up to such contempt? You know it well,
As do the watchful heavens above our heads,
Despite what these dissenters whisper round,
Nothing hath passed but strict and proper law
Against the treasonous nephews of Valerius.
What if his grief hath alienated his mind?
Must we endure his daily mockeries,
His sudden fits, his rage, and bitter taunts?
And now he posts his letters to the ghosts!
See, here "To Justice," and "To the Fates,"
This "To the Underworld," this to the sun.
Sweet broadsides to be plastered on our walls!
What is this but a libel on the crown,
Declaring our corruption to the mob?
A merry jest, is it not, gentlemen?
As if to say, there is no justice here.
But by my life, his counterfeit disease
Shall be no shield to these rebellions;
He and his faction soon shall know my rule
Is absolute; and if my justice sleeps,
He’ll wake it, and I'll strike the traitor down.
ISABELLA. My gracious sovereign, noble King Leontes,
The very sun and center of my heart,
Be patient with the old Valerius' faults;
They are the fruits of sorrow for his kin,
Whose bloody ends have pierced his ancient soul.
Far better to ignore his manic grief
Than punish an old warrior for his tears.
[Aside.] Thus must Isabella play the gentle dove.
But old Valerius, I have set the snare;
Once you are dead, and Roderigo safe,
The realm is ours, and all my fears are gone.
Enter Peasant.
How now, good fellow, seekest thou the court?
PEASANT. Yes, truly, if your ladyship be the queenship.
ISABELLA. I am the Queen, but yonder sits the King.
PEASANT. That's him. God and Saint George give you good morrow. I have brought you a parchment and a brace of capons here.
[Leontes reads the scroll.]
LEONTES. Guards, drag this villain out and sever his head!
PEASANT. How much is the reward for that?
ISABELLA. Come, fool, you are for the block.
PEASANT. The block! By my troth, I should have kept the capons and brought my own head in the basket. [Exit guarded.]
LEONTES. Outrageous and unbearable affronts!
Shall I sit by and stomach this abuse?
I know the architect of this foul jest.
He’d have the world believe his rebel kin,
Who lawfully were shortened by the axe,
Were slaughtered by my tyranny and hate!

VALERIUS. What, did you not drop straight down from the clouds?
PEASANT. The clouds? God save me, no. I've never been half that high, and I pray I never am. I’m just taking these capons to the lord justice. Got to settle a bloody mess of a fight between my cousin and the king's tax-gatherer.
CASSIUS. Brother, look no further. Here is the perfect vessel for your suit. Let him carry the birds to the King.
VALERIUS. Tell me, friend. Are you able to present a petition to the King with... a certain grace?
PEASANT. Grace? Well, sir, the only man I've ever petitioned is the tavern-keeper when the ale runs dry.
VALERIUS. Come closer, sirrah. No more dallying.
You shall give your plump birds to his Majesty,
And through my word, find favor at the court.
Here, take this copper for your trouble.
Give me the wax and seal. Now, tell me true:
Can you deliver my plea with a bold face?
PEASANT. Aye, my lord, I can.
VALERIUS. Then take this scroll. When you come before him, drop to your knees on the stones. Kiss his signet ring, hand over the birds, and wait for your great reward. I'll be close by. Do it without fear.
PEASANT. Never fear, sir. I know my business.
VALERIUS. Have you a gauntlet, sirrah? Let me see it.
Cassius, bind it up within the parchment,
For you have penned it like a groveling slave.
[To the Peasant]
When you have placed it in the King's own hands,
Find me at the tavern and tell me what he says.
PEASANT. God keep you, sir; I'm off. [Exit.]
VALERIUS. Come, Cassius. Lucius, attend me. [Exeunt.]
SCENE IV. The Royal Court.
Enter King Leontes, Queen Isabella, her sons Ferdinand and Roderigo, with Attendants. The King angrily grips several torn placards in his fist.
LEONTES. Look at this! Just look at it, my lords.
Was ever a sovereign of this realm
So openly abused? So mocked to his face?
And for what? For the simple execution of the law!
You know it well. The gods themselves know it.
No matter what these rebels whisper in the streets,
We followed strict and proper law
Against the treasonous nephews of Valerius.
Am I to excuse this because his grief has turned his wits?
Must we sit here and endure his daily mockeries,
His sudden frantic fits and bitter taunts?
Now he is sending letters to the ghosts!
Look here—"To Justice." "To the Fates."
This one "To the Underworld," and this to the bloody sun!
Sweet little broadsides to plaster on our walls.
What is this but a libel on the crown?
He is declaring us corrupt to the mob.
A merry jest, isn't it, gentlemen?
As if to say there is no justice left in Rome.
But by my life, his counterfeit madness
Will be no shield for this treason.
He and his rabble will soon learn that my rule
Is absolute. If my justice has been sleeping,
He has woken it. I will strike the traitor down.
ISABELLA. My gracious sovereign, my noble King...
The very sun and center of my heart.
Have patience with old Valerius.
These are just the bitter fruits of sorrow;
The bloody end of his kin has pierced his soul.
It is far better to ignore his manic grief
Than punish an old, broken warrior for his tears.
[Aside.]
So Isabella plays the gentle, cooing dove.
But old Valerius, I have already set the snare.
Once you are dead and Roderigo is safe,
The realm is finally ours. My fears go with you to the grave.
Enter Peasant.
How now, fellow? Do you seek the court?
PEASANT. Aye, truly, if your ladyship happens to be the queenship.
ISABELLA. I am the Queen. Yonder sits the King.
PEASANT. That's him? Right. God and Saint George give you good morrow. I've brought you a parchment and a brace of fat capons here.
[Leontes snatches the scroll and reads.]
LEONTES. Guards! Drag this villain to the block. Sever his head!
PEASANT. Right, and how much is the reward for that?
ISABELLA. Come along, fool. You are to be beheaded.
PEASANT. Beheaded! By my troth, I should have kept the birds and carried my own head in the basket. [Exit guarded.]
LEONTES. Outrageous! Unbearable affronts!
Am I expected to stomach this abuse?
I know exactly who built this foul jest.
He wants the world to think his rebel kin—
Who were lawfully shortened by the axe—
Were slaughtered by my tyranny and hate!

After much weeping, a verbal pact was sworn between us: first, that I would not squander the southern estate and would remain her lawful husband; secondly, that my visits to the city would never exceed a month without her blessing; thirdly, that I should not parade any permanent mistresses in our province; fourthly, in exchange for this, Elena Ivanovna permitted my liaisons with the local widows, provided it was done with the utmost discretion; fifthly, heaven forbid I should ever seek a formal separation; sixthly, should I—which God forbid—be struck by a genuine, consuming infatuation, I was obliged to confess it to her immediately. On this final point, however, Elena Ivanovna felt entirely secure. She possessed a coldly logical mind, and so she naturally viewed me as a jaded cynic, thoroughly incapable of true devotion. But a logical mind and a wounded vanity clash terribly, and that is precisely where our misery began. To judge certain individuals fairly, one must suspend ordinary moralizing and discard the conventional attitudes of society. I trust your intellect more than the judgment of the crowd. Perhaps you have already absorbed a great deal of scandalous nonsense regarding Elena Ivanovna. She certainly had her foolish eccentricities, but I tell you in all honesty that I genuinely regret the profound miseries into which I dragged her. Well, I suppose that is a sufficient eulogy for the most tolerant wife of a deeply intolerable husband. When she raged, I usually stared out the window and smoked in silence; this passive gentlemanly cruelty rarely failed to achieve its aim, and eventually, it even made her feel she had won. But your cousin she could not abide, at least not at the end. How she ever decided to bring such an extraordinary creature into our suffocating home as a music teacher, I will never understand. My only explanation is that Elena Ivanovna was a prone to sudden, dramatic obsessions and simply became enamored—platonically, of course—with your cousin. And who wouldn't? Just look at Natalya Pavlovna! I recognized the hazard instantly, and believe it or not, I resolved to feign complete blindness to her charms. But Natalya Pavlovna herself initiated the conversation, can you imagine? Would you also believe that Elena Ivanovna was genuinely furious with me at first for not echoing her rapturous, daily compliments about the girl? I don’t know what she expected! Naturally, Elena Ivanovna poured out all my darkest secrets to Natalya Pavlovna. She had a wretched compulsion to broadcast our private miseries to anyone who would listen; how could she spare her charming new confidante? They must have spent hours dissecting my character, and Natalya Pavlovna undoubtedly absorbed all those grotesque legends that circulate about my youth... I am willing to wager you have caught wind of them too?”
“I have. Ivanov claimed you drove a young cadet to suicide. Is that the truth?”
“Spare me those melodramatic inventions, I implore you,” said Grigory Ilyich, his face darkening with sudden revulsion. “If you are so desperate to wallow in that nonsense, I shall enlighten you another time, but for now...”
“They also spoke of a serf on your brother's estate whom you ordered beaten.”
“I ask you to abandon this line of questioning,” Grigory Ilyich cut in, his voice tightening with obvious impatience. “Was that the same serf whose ghost supposedly haunts my study?... You mentioned him yourself.”
Alexei's expression grew visibly darker. Grigory Ilyich studied his face, and Alexei thought he perceived a glimmer of malicious amusement in the older man's eyes. But Grigory Ilyich smoothed his features and replied with impeccable politeness:
“Yes, exactly. I perceive that your curiosity is quite inflamed, and I promise to gratify it fully when the moment is right. Good heavens! I realize that I must cut quite the tragic, romantic figure in the eyes of provincial society. Imagine my gratitude to Elena Ivanovna for feeding Natalya Pavlovna such lurid and captivating myths about my past. I cannot presume to know precisely how it affected her, but it undeniably served my purposes. Despite Natalya Pavlovna's innate purity and my own thoroughly repulsive demeanor—she was moved, at the very least, by a profound compassion for a ruined man.”

We had a pact, drawn up after endless tears. It went something like this: I was not to squander the southern estate, and I would remain her lawful husband. My trips to the city couldn't last more than a month unless she gave her blessing. I wasn't to parade any permanent mistresses around the province. In return? Elena Ivanovna would turn a blind eye to my little liaisons with the local widows, so long as I kept things utterly discreet. I was never, God forbid, to ask for a formal separation. And finally—should I ever be struck by a genuine, consuming passion (heaven help us)—I had to confess it to her at once.
She felt perfectly safe on that last point. Elena Ivanovna had a coldly logical mind, you see. To her, I was just a jaded cynic. Completely incapable of real devotion. But logic and wounded vanity are a terrible mix, and that is exactly where our misery started.
Look, to judge people fairly, you have to drop the usual moralizing. Forget how society looks at things. I trust your intellect over the crowd's, so I'll be frank. You’ve probably heard a lot of scandalous nonsense about my wife. She had her eccentricities, her foolish moments, yes. But honestly? I genuinely regret the profound misery I caused her. Well. Let that stand as a eulogy for the tolerant wife of a highly intolerable man.
Whenever she raged at me, I usually just stared out the window and smoked. Complete silence. It was a sort of passive, gentlemanly cruelty, and it almost always worked. Eventually, it even made her feel she’d won.
Your cousin, though. She couldn't abide your cousin—not at the end, anyway. How she ever brought such an extraordinary creature into our suffocating house to teach music is beyond me. My best guess? Elena Ivanovna was prone to these sudden, dramatic obsessions. She simply fell in love—platonically—with the girl. And who wouldn't? Look at Natalya Pavlovna!
I saw the danger the moment she arrived. Believe it or not, I actually resolved to play blind to her charms. But it was Natalya Pavlovna who took the first step. Can you imagine? And my wife—this is the best part—my wife was furious with me at first because I wouldn't join in her rapturous, daily praises of the girl. I don't know what she wanted from me!
Naturally, she spilled all my darkest secrets to Natalya. Elena Ivanovna had this wretched compulsion to broadcast our private miseries to anyone who'd listen. Why would she spare her charming new confidante? They must have spent hours dissecting me. Natalya Pavlovna undoubtedly absorbed every grotesque legend floating around about my youth. I'd wager you've caught wind of them, too?"
"I have," Alexei said. "Ivanov claimed you drove a young cadet to suicide. Is it true?"
"Spare me those melodramatic inventions," Grigory Ilyich muttered, his face darkening. "If you really must wallow in that nonsense, I'll enlighten you another time. But for now—"
"They also talked about a serf on your brother's estate. The one you had beaten."
"Drop it," Grigory Ilyich cut in, his patience snapping. "Is this the same serf whose ghost supposedly haunts my study? You brought him up yourself."
Alexei frowned, his expression hardening. Grigory Ilyich watched him closely. For a second, Alexei could have sworn he saw a flicker of malicious amusement in the older man's eyes, but it vanished instantly. Grigory Ilyich's face smoothed into a mask of perfect, chilling politeness.
"Yes, exactly. Your curiosity is clearly inflamed. Don't worry, I promise to gratify it fully when the moment is right. Good heavens! I'm starting to realize I must cut quite the tragic, romantic figure for the locals. Think of how grateful I should be to my wife! Feeding Natalya Pavlovna such lurid, captivating myths about my past. I can't guess exactly how it affected her, but it certainly served my purposes. In spite of her absolute purity—and my own thoroughly repulsive nature—she felt pity. At the very least, she felt profound compassion for a ruined man."

Alarum. Enter Titus.
TITUS. Lost, lost, all lost! I cannot look upon it.
The sapphire galley, the Phoenician pride,
With all her fifty vessels, turns the prow.
My sight is cursed to see it.
Enter Marcellus.
MARCELLUS. Gods and demons,
The entire host of them!
TITUS. What moves your rage?
MARCELLUS. The better fraction of the earth is thrown
To absolute folly. We have bartered away
Empires and territories.
TITUS. How stands the vanguard?
MARCELLUS. On our flank, like a doomed contagion,
Where ruin is assured. That painted sovereign of the East,
Whom plagues devour, in the very heat of war,
When victory stood balanced on a sword—
Or leaning to our favor—the panic takes her,
Like a frightened deer, raises the oars and flies.
TITUS. I saw the same.
My eyes recoiled from it and could not bear
To watch a moment more.
MARCELLUS. She once departed,
The shattered monument of her thrall, Valerius,
Spreads out his sails and, like a blinded gull,
Abandoning the clash at its very peak,
Speeds after her. I never witnessed a retreat
Of such disgrace. Wisdom, valor, discipline,
Never yet did so debase themselves.
TITUS. Alas, alas!
Enter Lucan.
LUCAN. Our destiny upon the waves is drowned
And sinks most bitterly. Had our commander
Been who he was born to be, we had prevailed.
Oh, he has set a pattern for our cowardice
Most vilely by his own!
TITUS. Aye, is your mind set there?
Why, then, the day is done.
LUCAN. Toward the northern straits they are escaped.
MARCELLUS. The wind is fair for it, and there I’ll wait
To see what follows.
LUCAN. To the usurper will I yield
My banners and my steel. Five captains already
Show me the path of treason.
TITUS. I’ll yet pursue
The broken fate of great Valerius,
Though my better sense cries out against me.
[Exeunt.]
SCENE XI. A Chamber in the Citadel.
Enter Valerius with attendants.
VALERIUS. Listen, the earth commands I tread no more upon its dirt.
It blushes to sustain me. Comrades, gather here.
I am so shadowed in this fading world that I
Have lost the sun forever. I have a vault
Bursting with silver. Take it, share it out. Flee,
And make your treaty with the foe.
ALL. We flee? Not us.
VALERIUS. I have run myself, and taught the bravest men
To turn and show their backs. Companions, go.
I have myself determined on a path
Which requires no escort. Begone.
My silver’s in the fortress. Take it. O,
I chased a sight that burns my eyes to see.
My very veins make war, for my old blood
Mocks my youthful fire, and they rebuke each other
For cowardice and lust. Good friends, depart.
You shall have seals from me to wealthy lords that will
Secure your safety. I pray you, do not weep,
Nor speak of your devotion. Take the cue
That my dark ruin offers. Leave the man
Who has forsaken himself. To the harbor, now.
I will bestow upon you ships and coin.
Give me some peace, I beg of you—I pray you, go;
Nay, do it; for truly I have lost the right to order.
Therefore I beg you. I will join you shortly.
[Sits down.]
Enter Xanthia led by Livia, Flavia, and Pindarus.
PINDARUS. Nay, gentle lady, go to him! Comfort him.
FLAVIA. Do, my sovereign.
LIVIA. Speak! What else remains?
XANTHIA. Let me rest here. O Fates!
VALERIUS. No, no, no, no, no.
PINDARUS. Do you see, my lord?
VALERIUS. O, shame, shame, shame!
LIVIA. Madam.
FLAVIA. Madam, O sweet queen!
PINDARUS. Lord, my lord!
VALERIUS. Yes, the conqueror, yes. He at the river kept
His blade like an ornament, while I hewed down
The fierce and seasoned Thracians, and 'twas I
That the wild rebels broke. He only traded
In his deputies, and had no footing
In the bloody dust of war. But now—it matters not.
XANTHIA. Ah, stand aside.
PINDARUS. The Queen, my lord, the Queen!
FLAVIA. Approach him, madam; give him words.
He is undone with sheer disgrace.
XANTHIA. Well then, support me. O!
PINDARUS. Most mighty general, stand. The Queen draws near.
Her face is fallen, and despair will claim her unless
Your pity grants a reprieve.
VALERIUS. I have murdered reputation,
A most unmanly betrayal.
PINDARUS. Sir, your Queen.
VALERIUS. O, where have you dragged me, Sovereign? See
How I must hide my ruin from your gaze,
By looking back at what I left behind,
Crushed in my disgrace.
XANTHIA. O my lord, my lord,
Forgive my trembling fleet! I never dreamed
That you would track my flight.
VALERIUS. Sovereign, you knew too well
My soul was to your canvas tethered tight,
And you would drag me with you.

“It appears a most disastrous turn of events.”
“You must speak to Clara at once. Inform her that you demand her compliance in this marriage.”
“Let her come down. I shall deliver my verdict.”
Mrs. Sterling tugged the bell-pull, and Miss Clara was requested in the study. “Step forward, my dear,” declared her father as she entered. “I have summoned you on a matter of the utmost gravity. I am told that Mr. Pendleton has requested your hand. Is this accurate?”
Clara confirmed that it was.
“Very good—and this proposal of marriage you have declined?”
“I have, father.”
“Very good. We arrive at the crux of the matter. Your mother insists that you accept him. Is that correct, Mrs. Sterling?”
“Indeed, or I shall cast her out entirely.”
“A tragic dilemma stands before you, Clara. From this moment, you must lose one of your parents. Your mother will cast you out if you do not marry Mr. Pendleton, and I shall do the same if you do.”
Clara struggled to suppress a laugh at such a sudden turn; but Mrs. Sterling, who had firmly believed her husband would act as she dictated, was profoundly outraged. “Whatever do you mean, Mr. Sterling, speaking in such a manner? You gave me your word to compel her to the match.”
“My dear,” her husband replied calmly, “I have but two modest requests. First, that you permit me the liberty of my own judgment in this affair; and, second, the liberty of my study. I should be most grateful to have the room to myself at your earliest convenience.”
Despite her fury at her husband, Mrs. Sterling did not abandon her campaign. She badgered Clara relentlessly, employing flattery and menace in equal measure. She attempted to recruit her eldest daughter, Mary, to her cause, but Mary gently refused to intervene; and Clara, fluctuating between grave sincerity and lighthearted amusement, withstood the barrage. Her tone shifted, but her resolve remained absolute. Mr. Pendleton, in the interim, was brooding alone over the morning’s events. He held too high an opinion of his own consequence to understand why his cousin might reject him; and though his vanity was bruised, his heart was entirely unaffected. His affection for her had been merely theoretical, and the prospect of her incurring her mother’s wrath stifled any lingering disappointment.
Amidst this domestic upheaval, Miss Eleanor Hayes arrived to call upon the family. She was intercepted in the hall by Beatrice, who rushed forward and whispered excitedly, “I am so thrilled you are here, for we are in such an uproar! What do you suppose occurred this morning? Mr. Pendleton offered for Clara, and she flatly refused him.”
Eleanor had scarce a moment to react before they were met by Sarah, who eagerly repeated the tale. Upon entering the sitting room, where Mrs. Sterling sat in isolation, the matriarch instantly seized upon the topic, pleading with Miss Hayes to pity her and to sway her obstinate friend to obey her family’s commands. “I implore you, dear Miss Hayes,” she lamented with a theatrical sigh; “no one supports my cause, no one champions my side; I am treated dreadfully, and no one considers my fragile constitution.”
The entrance of Mary and Clara spared Eleanor from answering. “Ah, here she is,” Mrs. Sterling carried on, “appearing entirely unbothered, caring no more for our survival than if we resided in London, so long as her own whims are satisfied. But heed my words, Miss Clara, if you persist in dismissing every eligible suitor in this fashion, you shall never secure a match—and I cannot fathom who will support you when your father passes. I certainly shall not have the means, so consider yourself warned. I wash my hands of you from this moment. I stated in the study that I would never speak to you again, and I am a woman of my word. I find no joy in conversing with ungrateful offspring. Truthfully, I find little joy in conversing with anyone at all. Those who endure such nervous afflictions as I do possess very little appetite for conversation.”

"It's a complete catastrophe, there's no other word for it."
"Then speak to Clara yourself! Tell her you absolutely demand she marry him."
"By all means, send for the girl. She can hear my verdict right now."
Mrs. Sterling violently yanked the bell-pull. A minute later, Clara stepped into the study.
"Come in, Clara," her father said, leaning back in his leather armchair. "We have a matter of grave importance to settle. Word has reached me that Mr. Pendleton asked for your hand this morning. True?"
Clara nodded. "It is."
"Right. And you turned him down?"
"I did, Father."
"Splendid. That brings us to the crux of the issue. Your mother here insists you accept the man. Isn't that right, Mrs. Sterling?"
"Yes! Or I'll never look at her again."
Mr. Sterling sighed heavily. "Well, Clara, you face a grim dilemma. As of today, you are destined to lose a parent. Your mother refuses to ever see you again if you do not marry Mr. Pendleton. And I refuse to see you again if you do."
Clara couldn't help but smile at the trap he'd sprung. Mrs. Sterling, however, had spent the last hour convinced her husband was entirely on her side. She stared at him in sheer outrage.
"What on earth are you talking about, Mr. Sterling? You gave me your word you would force the match!"
"My dear," he said smoothly, "I only ask two small favors of you today. The first is that you allow me to use my own brain. The second is that you allow me the use of my study. I'd very much like the room to myself now."
Being dismissed did nothing to stop Mrs. Sterling. She spent the rest of the afternoon badgering Clara, bouncing wildly between sweet coaxing and bitter threats. She tried to rope her eldest daughter, Mary, into the fight, but Mary politely refused to take the bait. Clara just weathered the storm. Sometimes she answered seriously, sometimes she teased her mother back. Her mood shifted, but her answer never changed.
As for Mr. Pendleton, he spent the afternoon brooding alone. He thought far too highly of his own charm to understand how anyone could reject him. His pride was certainly bruised, but his heart was fine. He'd only ever liked the idea of Clara anyway. Plus, knowing she was about to face the full force of her mother’s wrath made him feel a lot better about the whole thing.
Right in the middle of all this chaos, Eleanor Hayes came to call.
Beatrice caught her in the hallway before she could even take off her bonnet. "Oh, thank goodness you're here, it's a madhouse!" she stage-whispered. "You'll never guess. Pendleton proposed to Clara this morning, and she told him no!"
Before Eleanor could even process the gossip, Sarah bounded out to repeat it. They dragged Eleanor into the sitting room, where Mrs. Sterling was wallowing alone.
The older woman immediately lunged for sympathy. "Eleanor, thank God. Please, talk some sense into her! Nobody in this house cares about me. I'm treated appallingly, and not one of them gives a thought to my blood pressure."
Clara and Mary walked in just then, saving Eleanor from having to think of a polite response.
"Oh, look at her," Mrs. Sterling spat. "Waltzing in without a care in the world. She'd watch us all starve as long as she gets her way. Listen to me, Clara—if you keep turning your nose up at perfectly good men, you're going to die an old maid. And I certainly won't have the money to keep you once your father is dead! So consider this your warning. I wash my hands of you. I told you in the study I'd never speak to you again, and I meant it. I despise talking to ungrateful children. In fact, I despise talking to anyone today. My nerves are completely shattered, and I'm in no mood for conversation."

VALERIUS. Do you perceive the scandal?
CASSIUS. I cannot wish the scandal undone, the fruit of it being so handsome.
VALERIUS. Yet I have a lawful heir, sir, some year older than this boy, who is no more cherished in my heart. Though this rogue slipped into the world before the priest gave leave, his mother was a beauty; there was much merriment at his forging, and the bastard must be claimed. Do you know this valiant youth, Marcus?
MARCUS. I do not, my lord.
VALERIUS. My Lord of Cassius: hold him henceforth as my esteemed friend.
MARCUS. My humble duty to your lordship.
CASSIUS. I must embrace you, and hope to know you better.
MARCUS. Sir, I shall strive to be worthy.
VALERIUS. He hath been abroad these nine years, and shall depart again. The King approaches.
[Trumpets sound.]
Enter King Leontes, Duke of Milan, Duke of Venice, Isabella, Beatrice, Diana, and Attendants.
LEONTES. Escort the lords of Aragon and Naples, Valerius.
VALERIUS. I shall, your grace.
[Exeunt Valerius and Marcus.]
LEONTES. In the interim, we shall reveal our secret design. Fetch the map. Let it be known we have severed our empire into three. It is our firm resolve to cast the heavy burdens of state from our tired shoulders, bestowing them on younger backs, while we crawl unencumbered to our grave. Our son of Milan, and you, our equally beloved son of Venice, we intend this very hour to announce our daughters' respective inheritances, so that future bloodshed may be thwarted now. The princes of Aragon and Naples, fierce rivals for our youngest daughter's hand, have tarried long in our court and demand their answer. Tell me, my daughters—since we now strip ourselves of power, lands, and the cares of the crown—which of you shall we say loves us the most? We wish to deal our greatest generosity where natural affection and merit align. Isabella, our firstborn, speak first.
ISABELLA. Sire, I love you beyond what mere words can measure; more dearly than my own sight, the open air, or my freedom. Beyond all treasures, however rare or costly; as much as life itself, matched with health, grace, and honor. As deeply as any child has ever loved, or any father has ever been loved. A devotion that renders my breath short and my tongue tied; beyond all limits of comparison, I love you.
DIANA. [Aside.] What shall Diana say? Love, and keep silent.
LEONTES. Of all these borders, from this river to this valley, rich with dark woods and fertile plains, with abundant streams and sprawling pastures, we make you sovereign. To your and Milan's heirs, let this be everlasting. What says our second daughter, our beloved Beatrice, wife of Venice? Speak.
BEATRICE. Sire, I am forged of the exact same steel as my sister, and value myself at her equal. In my honest heart, I find she has spoken the very shape of my love; only she falls short, in that I declare myself a sworn enemy to every other earthly joy that the senses can perceive, and find my only true happiness in your royal affection.
DIANA. [Aside.] Then poor Diana. And yet, not truly poor, for my love is far heavier than my hollow words.
LEONTES. To you and your heirs forever we bequeath this wealthy third of our grand realm; no less in scope, revenue, and beauty than what was granted to Isabella. Now, our final joy, though the youngest and smallest; for whose tender affections the vineyards of Aragon and the gold of Naples bitterly compete. What can you say to claim a third more magnificent than your sisters'? Speak.
DIANA. Nothing, my lord.
LEONTES. Nothing?
DIANA. Nothing.
LEONTES. Nothing will be born of nothing. Speak again.
DIANA. Wretched as I am, I cannot drag my heart into my mouth. I love your majesty according to my duty; no more, and no less.
LEONTES. How now, Diana? Fix your speech a little, lest you ruin your own fortune.
DIANA. My good liege, you fathered me, raised me, and loved me. I return those obligations exactly as is proper: I obey you, I love you, and I honor you above all. Why do my sisters have husbands if they claim they love only you? Perhaps, when I take a husband, that lord who accepts my vows shall take half my love with him, and half my care and duty. I am certain I shall never wed like my sisters, to give my father all my love.
LEONTES. But does your heart truly agree with this?
DIANA. It does, my good lord.
LEONTES. So young, and so hard?
DIANA. So young, my lord, and truthful.

VALERIUS: Do you smell the scandal?

DIANA: Nothing.

She commands Prudence to be constantly vigilant in calculating future yields, ensuring that Wealth may increase and remain secure. Justice she directs to settle all debts and observe contracts strictly, not from a love of fairness, but to avoid the penalties of the magistrate and to secure the commercial alliances necessary for acquiring more gold. Fortitude she orders to endure the bitterest colds and the most exhausting labors, keeping the mind firmly fixed on the promise of future profits, so that the pain of present toil is swallowed up by the anticipation of a full treasury. Temperance she restricts to a frugal diet and modest apparel, lest excessive spending diminish the hoard, and thus Wealth, which the avaricious elevate as the highest good, be tragically diminished. Thus the noble virtues, stripped of their inherent majesty, are reduced to the grovelling servants of Wealth, acting as the slaves of a cruel and barren master. There is nothing, our scholars argue, more degraded and perverse than this arrangement, nor anything that right-minded men should find more repulsive. And they are entirely correct. But I do not believe this hierarchy would be any more acceptable even if the virtues were made subservient to Intellectual Prestige; for although that prestige is not a crass material obsession, it is nevertheless bloated with arrogance and overflowing with vanity. Therefore, it is beneath the eternal dignity of the virtues to be yoked to academic pride, wherein Prudence learns nothing, Justice balances nothing, and Temperance restrains nothing, except to ensure that a man is lauded by his peers and his own ego is inflated. Nor can such men acquit themselves of hypocrisy when they, claiming to despise the applause of the crowd, retreat inward to admire their own intellects, feeling entirely satisfied with themselves. For this supposed virtue—if it can be called virtue at all—is merely another form of human flattery; he who seeks to impress himself is still bowing to a man. Conversely, the man of genuine piety, whose faith and love are anchored in God, focuses his scrutiny on his own spiritual poverty, finding displeasure in himself so that he might find pleasure in the truth. He attributes whatever goodness he possesses not to his own intellect, but to the mercy of Him whom he reverences, offering gratitude for the afflictions that have been healed, and ceaselessly praying for the purification of those that remain unhealed.
	•	That worldly prosperity is distributed by Him who holds all authority, and by whose divine plan the universe is governed.
This being the case, we do not ascribe the accumulation of national wealth and the flourishing of empires to anyone other than the true God. He reserves the eternal treasures of heaven exclusively for the righteous, yet He distributes earthly riches to both the righteous and the wicked, according to His own will, which is always perfectly just. For although we may apprehend fragments of the design by which He governs the world, whenever He chooses to reveal them, it is ultimately beyond our finite capacity to dissect the secret motives of human hearts and perfectly measure why one kingdom prospers while another falls into ruin. Therefore, the one true God, who never abandons humanity to absolute chaos but provides just governance, granted immense riches and maritime supremacy to the Carthaginians when He saw fit, just as He did to the Greeks, and even to the Egyptians, who, as their own monuments demonstrate, worshipped beasts and rivers—to say nothing of the Hebrew people, whom I have already discussed, who worshipped only the true God during their time of sovereignty. The same God, therefore, who gave the Egyptians overflowing harvests, though they did not offer sacrifices to the goddess Ceres, and who bestowed the treasures of the earth, though they did not venerate the many spirits which the Romans believed to preside over the soil—He, I affirm, granted them economic dominion, despite their absolute ignorance of the very deities to whom other nations foolishly attributed their prosperity.

Picture Avarice giving orders to the virtues. She tells Prudence to stay awake at night, obsessively calculating market shifts just to keep the vault secure. She tells Justice to pay debts and honor contracts—not out of any real sense of fairness, but simply to dodge the magistrate and keep the trade routes open. Then there is Fortitude. Avarice orders Fortitude to suffer through freezing winters and exhausting labor, using the sheer promise of future profits to numb the pain of the present work. And Temperance? She is commanded to eat scraps and wear threadbare coats, terrified that a single lavish meal might shrink the hoard.
In this grotesque scenario, the virtues are stripped of their majesty. They become nothing more than accountants and bodyguards for Wealth. It’s a degrading picture. Scholars rightly call it perverse, something any decent person should find repulsive.
But honestly, it isn't much better if we swap out Wealth for Intellectual Prestige. Sure, academic pride isn't a crass material obsession, but it’s still bloated with arrogance. It is entirely beneath the dignity of the virtues to serve as handmaids to vanity. Why should Prudence study, or Temperance practice restraint, if the only goal is to win an argument or stroke one's own ego? Even the introverted scholars who claim to despise public applause aren't immune. They retreat to their studies to admire their own minds. But flattering yourself is still flattering a human being.
True piety looks entirely different. A person anchored in God looks inward and sees their own poverty. They aren't trying to please themselves; they want to align with the truth. Whatever goodness they find in their character, they credit to God's grace, not their own brilliance. They give thanks for the parts of their soul that have been healed, and they pray over the broken parts that remain.
Worldly prosperity comes only from the true authority that governs the universe.
Because of this, we cannot credit the rise of wealthy empires to anyone but the true God. He saves the actual treasures of heaven for the righteous, but He hands out earthly money and power to both the good and the wicked. He does this exactly as He pleases, and His reasons are always just, even when they baffle us. We might catch glimpses of His grand design here and there. But overall? It is completely beyond our mental capacity to untangle the hidden motives in human hearts and figure out exactly why one nation gets rich while another goes bankrupt.
The one true God never abandons the world to chaos. He gave immense wealth and maritime dominance to the Carthaginians when He felt like it. He did the same for the Greeks and the Egyptians, even though the Egyptians worshipped crocodiles and the Nile. (This leaves aside the Israelites, who actually worshipped the true God while they had their kingdom). God gave the Egyptians bumper crops, even though they never bothered to pray to Ceres. He gave them gold from the earth, even though they didn't worship the spirits of the soil. He handed them absolute economic dominance, regardless of the fact that they completely ignored the false gods other nations insisted were responsible for such blessings.

With such accounts of their lively gatherings and pleasantries did Florence, aided by the prompting of her younger sister, occupy her companions all the way to Willowbourne. Eleanor tuned them out as best she could, but there was no escaping the frequent utterance of Carter’s name. Their welcome at the manor was exceptionally warm. Mrs. Crawford rejoiced to see Jane looking as radiant as ever; and more than once during the meal did Mr. Crawford remark to Eleanor,——
“I am exceedingly glad you have returned, Eleanor.”
Their gathering in the dining-room was considerable, for nearly all the neighboring Winslows came to greet the travelers and hear the tidings; and various were the topics that engaged them: Lady Winslow was inquiring of Jane, across the table, regarding the health and gardens of her married sister; Mrs. Crawford was doubly occupied, on one side gathering a report of the latest metropolitan fashions from Jane, and on the other, relaying them to the younger Miss Winslows; and Florence, in a tone far more piercing than anyone else’s, was detailing the morning's exploits to whomever would listen.
“Oh, Beatrice,” said she, “I do wish you had accompanied us, for it was such sport! As we rode along we drew all the curtains tight, and pretended the carriage was entirely vacant; and I would have kept it so the entire journey, if my sister had not felt faint; and when we stopped at the Red Lion, I believe we acted very generously, for we treated the others to the most delightful pastries imaginable, and had you been there, we should have treated you as well. And leaving the inn was just as riotous! I thought we should never manage to climb back into the carriage. I was overcome with merriment. And then we were so boisterous the whole way home! We chattered and laughed with such volume that the whole county might have heard us!”
To this, Beatrice very solemnly answered, “Far be it from me, my dear sister, to disparage such amusements. They are undoubtedly suited to the general disposition of youthful minds. But I must admit they hold no attraction for me. I should vastly prefer the quiet study of a volume.”
But Florence did not register a single word of this rebuttal. She rarely paid attention to anyone for more than a moment, and never heeded Beatrice under any circumstance. In the afternoon Florence was insistent that the young ladies should stroll down to the village, and observe what the townsfolk were about; but Eleanor firmly resisted the proposal. It must not be rumored that the Crawford sisters could not abide at home for half a day before seeking out the militia officers. There was an additional motive, too, for her refusal. She deeply dreaded crossing paths with Carter again, and was determined to evade him for as long as she could. The relief she felt at the regiment’s impending departure was beyond description. In less than a month they would march away, and once they were gone, she prayed there would be no further distress on his account. She had not been situated at home for very long before she perceived that the seaside scheme, which Florence had alluded to at the inn, was the subject of constant debate between her parents. Eleanor recognized instantly that her father had no intention of conceding; but his responses were simultaneously so evasive and ambiguous, that her mother, though frequently frustrated, never abandoned the hope of ultimate triumph.
