(CNN)A Canadian provincial lawmaker has requested a formal review of legislative rules regarding childcare, after she was informed she could no longer bring her infant daughter into the assembly chamber.
Sarah Lin, a Member of the Legislative Assembly (MLA) in British Columbia for the opposition party, said she was told by the Sergeant-at-Arms that it was a violation of parliamentary decorum to bring a child to a voting session after she attended with her daughter on Thursday. Lin shared a memorandum sent to her by the clerk's office, which referenced standing orders updated last year: "Non-members, including dependents, may not occupy a seat on the floor during official proceedings," adding that this also applied to committee rooms during broadcasted debates.
The Speaker of the House has asked the Committee on Parliamentary Privilege to examine the guidelines surrounding infants in the legislature following the incident, national broadcasters reported.
"Working mothers are told to lean in, but are literally locked out of the room it seems..." Lin wrote on Twitter following the incident.
"My daughter is barely two months old, so I cannot leave her at home, and the building does not offer drop-in childcare. So I am in an impossible situation," Lin told a local news network on Friday.
"I need to be present to represent my constituents, but I also have a nursing infant who relies on me. I was told unequivocally that the legislature considers the presence of a baby a breach of decorum and a distraction to the assembly.
"Yet, we routinely excuse members taking loud phone calls in the gallery. It feels like these rules were designed for a time when politics was exclusively the domain of older men of independent means with stay-at-home spouses," she added.
A spokesperson for the Legislative Assembly told CNN in an email that it is essential for all elected officials to participate fully in parliamentary duties without disruption.
"Members are encouraged to work with the Speaker's office and administrative staff to accommodate their needs while maintaining the formal environment of the chamber," the spokesperson said. "We are currently in active discussions with MLA Lin regarding this matter."
Lin noted that while she would never bring her active four-year-old son to a debate because "he would undoubtedly disrupt the proceedings and run down the aisles within five minutes," her newborn was "fast asleep and completely silent."
Referencing the updated standing orders, Lin, who had previously brought her first child to caucus meetings without issue, stated: "I fail to see what has changed. What is clear is that institutional barriers remain for young parents entering politics, and that ultimately hurts our democratic representation."
Lin previously clashed with the legislative administrative board earlier this year when her request for a remote proxy vote during her designated maternity leave was denied.
Responding to a conservative commentator's suggestion on Monday that she should "stay home and focus on being a mother," Lin tweeted: "Without a proxy system, my district goes unrepresented if I stay home. I am doing both my jobs."
In the US, Senator Tammy Duckworth made history in 2018 as she became the first senator to cast a vote on the Senate floor with her newborn by her side, days after the Senate changed longstanding rules to allow newborns onto the Senate floor during votes.
Similarly, in 2019, New Zealand MP Tāmati Coffey brought his six-week-old son into the debating chamber, where the baby was famously cradled by the house speaker. Prime Minister Jacinda Ardern also made headlines in 2018 when she brought her three-month-old daughter to the United Nations General Assembly.
However, the presence of children in political arenas has often sparked backlash. In 2015, European Parliament member Licia Ronzulli faced mixed reactions after frequently voting with her daughter in a sling, highlighting the ongoing global debate over work-life balance in high-level politics.

A Canadian lawmaker is pushing back against what she calls "archaic" workplace rules after being barred from bringing her newborn onto the legislative floor. 
British Columbia MLA Sarah Lin was reprimanded by the Sergeant-at-Arms on Thursday for bringing her two-month-old daughter to a voting session. Following the incident, Lin received a formal memo from the clerk’s office pointing to a recently updated standing order: "Non-members, including dependents, may not occupy a seat on the floor during official proceedings." The ban, she was informed, also extends to broadcasted committee rooms. 
The clash has prompted the Speaker of the House to ask the Committee on Parliamentary Privilege to review the assembly's childcare guidelines.
Taking to Twitter shortly after being told to leave, Lin didn't mince words. "Working mothers are told to lean in, but are literally locked out of the room," she wrote.
Speaking to local news networks on Friday, Lin highlighted the impossible bind she found herself in. With no drop-in childcare available at the legislature and a nursing infant who can't be left at home, she felt completely cornered. "I need to be present to represent my constituents, but I also have a nursing infant who relies on me," she said.
Lin found it particularly frustrating that a sleeping baby is viewed as a breach of decorum, pointing out that lawmakers frequently disrupt proceedings with loud phone calls without facing any consequences. To her, the rule smacks of an outdated political culture built around "older men with stay-at-home spouses."
While a spokesperson for the Legislative Assembly released a standard statement emphasizing the need to "accommodate needs while maintaining the formal environment," they confirmed they are in active discussions with Lin to find a solution.
For her part, Lin drew a sharp distinction between bringing her newborn and her energetic four-year-old son, who she joked would "undoubtedly disrupt the proceedings and run down the aisles." The infant, however, was completely silent. Having brought her first child to caucus meetings years ago without any pushback, she questioned what had actually changed—other than the enforcement of institutional barriers that actively keep young parents out of politics.
This isn't Lin's first fight over family-friendly policies. Earlier this year, she unsuccessfully petitioned the administrative board to allow remote proxy voting during her maternity leave. When a conservative pundit recently suggested she should just stay home and focus on motherhood, she quickly fired back online. Without a proxy system, she noted, staying home means her district loses its voice entirely.
The debate over babies in legislative chambers is hardly unique to Canada. In 2018, U.S. Senator Tammy Duckworth made history by casting a vote with her newborn after successfully lobbying to change Senate rules. New Zealand’s Parliament has also embraced the shift; in 2019, MP Tāmati Coffey brought his infant to a debate, and the baby was happily cradled by the Speaker of the House.
Still, progress is uneven. Politicians like European Parliament member Licia Ronzulli have faced both praise and heavy criticism for bringing their kids to work, proving that the global debate over balancing high-level politics with motherhood is far from settled.

(CNN)Barcelona will play Arsenal in a star-studded Europa League knockout round tie after fixtures were redrawn following a software malfunction.
European football's governing body UEFA declared the initial draw void earlier on Monday after Juventus was drawn by mistake and the Italian Serie A club will probably be relieved. Juventus was initially drawn against Barcelona but will now face a slightly easier two-legged tie against Portuguese side Sporting CP -- although Ruben Amorim's side is a formidable force in Portugal.
Europa League knockout draw Sevilla vs. PSV Eindhoven Sporting CP vs. Juventus Bayer Leverkusen vs. Monaco Ajax vs. Union Berlin Shakhtar Donetsk vs. Rennes FC Salzburg vs. AS Roma Manchester United vs. Real Betis Barcelona vs. Arsenal
However, it means fans will be denied the mouthwatering prospect of watching Robert Lewandowski renew his rivalry with Juventus -- for now at least.
The Catalan side will instead face the tricky prospect of playing current Premier League leader Arsenal. Mikel Arteta's team is one of the competition's most dynamic sides and has been in good form domestically of late.
It will also see Pierre-Emerick Aubameyang, a former captain of Arsenal, return to the Emirates Stadium.
Héctor Bellerín, Arsenal's former defender, will also return to face the club where he won multiple FA Cup titles. The defender moved to Barcelona at the start of the season but has seen limited action due to tactical choices.
Elsewhere, reigning Conference League champion AS Roma will face Austrian side FC Salzburg -- the only fixture that remained the same from the initial draw.
Last year's quarter-finalist Manchester United will face Real Betis as it searches for its second Europa League trophy as six-time winner Sevilla takes on PSV Eindhoven. Meanwhile, one of the tournament favorites Ajax will face Union Berlin while impressive Bundesliga side Bayer Leverkusen faces Monaco.
The first legs of the knockout ties are scheduled for February 16 and 23 with the returns scheduled for March 9 and 16. The final is set to take place in Budapest, Hungary, on May 31.

A massive software glitch at UEFA headquarters forced a complete redraw of the Europa League knockout stage on Monday. The result? A heavyweight clash between Barcelona and Arsenal.
The initial draw was scrapped entirely after Juventus was improperly placed in the wrong pot. The Italian giants are probably breathing a sigh of relief, though. They were originally slated to play Barcelona, but thanks to the redraw, they've drawn Portugal's Sporting CP. It's a slightly softer landing, even if Sporting has proven to be a tough out in Europe lately.

The matchup also comes with plenty of built-in drama. Pierre-Emerick Aubameyang will be making his highly anticipated return to the Emirates Stadium to face his former club. He'll be joined by Héctor Bellerín, who spent years flying down the right flank for Arsenal before heading to Camp Nou this season, though he's struggled to find consistent minutes under Xavi.
Looking at the rest of the bracket, Jose Mourinho’s AS Roma—fresh off winning the inaugural Conference League—drew FC Salzburg. Unbelievably, that was the only matchup that survived the voided first draw.
Manchester United is set to battle Real Betis as the Red Devils chase another European trophy, while tournament specialists Sevilla face off against PSV Eindhoven. Rounding out the heavy hitters, a talented Ajax side takes on Union Berlin, and Bayer Leverkusen gets Monaco in what promises to be a highly entertaining tie.
The opening legs will kick off on February 16 and 23, with the return fixtures locked in for March 9 and 16. The tournament concludes with the final in Budapest on May 31.

(CNN)At least 14 people died when their inflatable dinghy capsized off the Spanish island of Gran Canaria in the Atlantic Ocean on Tuesday, in the latest maritime tragedy involving migrants this month, the country's maritime rescue service said.
Authorities launched a frantic search and rescue mission overnight south of Gran Canaria. Helicopters and rescue vessels were dispatched to the region, the rescue agency said.
Spain is one of the primary entry points into the European Union for migrants and asylum seekers from West Africa and the Maghreb, though the dangerous Atlantic route has seen a surge in traffic since 2019, as patrols increased in the Mediterranean.
The country's EFE news agency reported that the bodies of 10 men, three women, and a toddler were retrieved from the water.
Seventy-five people were suspected to have been aboard the vessel, which according to the rescue service was believed to have departed from the coast of Western Sahara.
The exact reasons for the vessel overturning remained unknown.
Fernando Grande-Marlaska, Spain's interior minister, stated that human smuggling networks were to blame for the fatalities.
The smuggling networks "show zero regard for human safety, packing dozens of vulnerable individuals, lacking flotation devices, into flimsy craft completely unsuited for the open ocean," he said in a press release.
Earlier Tuesday, Spanish officials reported they had retrieved eight bodies following a separate shipwreck, when a wooden boat transporting migrants sank near the coast of Tenerife on Monday.
Another 82 individuals were rescued during that mission. The maritime rescue service stated preliminary reports indicated those migrants had departed from Senegal.
Over the weekend, a dinghy estimated to have been carrying up to 60 migrants deflated off the coast of Fuerteventura, leaving several dozen people unaccounted for.

Fourteen people are dead after their inflatable dinghy flipped off the coast of Gran Canaria on Tuesday. According to Spain's maritime rescue service, it is just the latest in a string of deadly migrant crossings in the Atlantic this month.
Emergency crews scrambled to launch a search and rescue operation south of the island overnight, deploying both helicopters and patrol boats to scour the waters.
For migrants fleeing West Africa and the Maghreb, Spain remains a major gateway into the European Union. With authorities heavily policing the Mediterranean, the notoriously treacherous Atlantic route has seen a massive spike in crossings since 2019.
Local news agency EFE confirmed the recovery of 14 bodies: ten men, three women, and a toddler.
Rescue officials suspect the dinghy departed from the coast of Western Sahara carrying roughly 75 people. It is still not entirely clear what caused the boat to capsize in the open ocean.
Spain's Interior Minister, Fernando Grande-Marlaska, placed the blame squarely on human smuggling rings. In a press release, he condemned the networks for showing "zero regard for human safety," accusing them of packing vulnerable people into flimsy, unseaworthy vessels without any life jackets.
The Gran Canaria disaster capped off a grim 48 hours for the region. Just hours earlier on Tuesday, Spanish officials confirmed they had pulled eight bodies from the water after a wooden migrant boat sank near Tenerife. While 82 people were pulled to safety in that operation, preliminary reports suggest the vessel had originally set sail from Senegal.
This comes just days after another tragedy over the weekend, when a separate dinghy carrying an estimated 60 migrants deflated off the coast of Fuerteventura. Dozens still remain missing.

New York (CNN Business)For many years, the world's most popular technology stocks have been the so-called FAANGs: Facebook, Amazon, Apple, Netflix and Google. But given that Facebook (now Meta) and Netflix have seen massive shifts in their market dominance following recent economic changes, it might be time for investors to stop lumping all of the tech giants together.
"The FAANGs had their day in the sun and that has faded," said Sarah Lin, senior analyst at Horizon Wealth.
Several major US fund managers have reduced legacy social media stocks from portfolios to a fraction of their former weight. Trading shares of several leading consumer tech companies, has seen heavy volatility amid shifting user habits. And the broader tech sector has been under pressure since late 2021, the year after the pandemic boom.
Tech could face further corrections within months
"The idea that a company as large as Meta can lose so much value is a big deal," Lin said.
Read More
It seems likely that FAANG will not be the primary driver of top tech funds anytime soon. Even for those retail traders still willing to invest in legacy tech assets, it's not clear what comes next.
"Some investors are asking about exposure to artificial intelligence in tech funds. With indexes starting to rebalance away from the old guard, it's still a wait-and-see game," said Marcus Thorne, head of equities at Vanguard Partners.
For investors looking to get exposure to big tech, Lin said, each company should be looked at individually.
"The name of the game will be differentiation. Don't invest in tech based on an acronym," Lin said. "It's always strange to say that Apple and Netflix are the same thing, for example. They're not."
Forget the FAANGs and look at MATANA or the Magnificent Seven?
To stick with the acronyms, the inclusion of Microsoft and Nvidia has essentially turned the FAANGs into MATANA (Microsoft, Apple, Tesla, Alphabet, Nvidia, Amazon) — and that could be a permanent change, said David Vance, managing partner with IndexIQ, a global index provider.
"Will investors ever embrace the old FAANG again? If growth slows, it's a moot point. But it's also hard to believe that people will ignore AI anytime soon," Vance told CNN Business.
Vance said some investors may start to look at other tech sectors to replace streaming and social media, such as cloud computing and semiconductor manufacturing. The FAANG could become the FATANG. He added that AMD, Broadcom and Salesforce are intriguing, as are Adobe and Oracle. You could dub Microsoft, Nvidia, Apple and Tesla the MNAT markets.
"People love their acronyms," Vance joked.
Of course, any tech market in consumer software — think social media — is inherently risky because of how close it is financially to digital ad-spending cycles. So other ad-reliant companies may be a hard sell for institutional investors.
AI boom has sparked a scramble for semiconductors
Other experts argue that investors are looking more at individual companies in tech and less at the sectors themselves.
"A typical investor sees big tech as a class of stocks. It's a piece of a portfolio," said Elena Rostova, US investment analyst at TradeNet.
"The tech landscape has been changing for awhile," Rostova added, noting that many investors have been skeptical of social media since major privacy updates in 2021. "There has been more anxiety."

For a long time, the FAANG acronym—Facebook, Amazon, Apple, Netflix, and Google—ruled Wall Street. They were the undisputed heavyweights of the tech sector. But with Meta's digital ad struggles and Netflix's subscriber volatility, throwing all these mega-caps into the exact same bucket doesn't make much sense anymore.
"FAANG really had its moment, but that era is fading," explains Sarah Lin, a senior analyst at Horizon Wealth.
The market is already pricing in the shift. Legacy tech darlings are facing brutal downgrades, and Wall Street is waking up to the fact that not all tech giants are created equal. The fact that the broader tech sector has been under pressure since the post-pandemic boom of late 2021 has only accelerated this trend.
"Seeing a titan like Meta shed that much market cap so quickly was a real wake-up call," Lin noted.
So, what happens to the tech-heavy mutual funds and ETFs that relied heavily on these five pillars? It's complicated.
"Clients are definitely asking about how to pivot their tech exposure," says Marcus Thorne, head of equities at Vanguard Partners. "Right now, it's a lot of wait-and-see as indexes gradually rebalance away from the old guard."
Lin's advice to investors is simple: look at the fundamentals of the individual company, not the catchy acronym. "Differentiation is everything right now. You can't just buy a basket of tech stocks based on a buzzword," she said. "Lumping Apple's hardware empire in with Netflix's streaming model just because they're both 'tech' is completely absurd."
If the market still demands an acronym, it might be time to adopt MATANA (Microsoft, Apple, Tesla, Alphabet, Nvidia, and Amazon) or simply embrace the "Magnificent Seven."
Adding AI powerhouse Nvidia and software giant Microsoft to the mix reflects a permanent shift in market leadership, according to David Vance, managing partner at IndexIQ. "Are people going to blindly buy the old FAANG group again? Probably not," Vance told CNN Business. "Growth is happening elsewhere, particularly in artificial intelligence, and money follows growth."
Vance pointed out that investors are actively hunting for replacements in cloud computing and semiconductor manufacturing. While he jokes that "Wall Street just loves a good acronym," he notes that enterprise companies like AMD, Broadcom, and Salesforce are drawing serious attention from institutional money.
But betting on consumer-facing tech remains a highly risky play, especially for companies tied directly to volatile digital ad spending.
Ultimately, the prevailing strategy has moved from buying the entire sector to picking the right horse. "Investors still view mega-cap tech as a crucial portfolio building block," says Elena Rostova, a US investment strategist at TradeNet. "But the landscape is fracturing. People have been nervous about social media stocks since the big privacy updates in 2021, and that anxiety is forcing investors to be much more selective today."

It is widely accepted that offering flexible working hours can positively impact employee performance. However, there are various other strategies that businesses can adopt to enhance job satisfaction in the workplace. In this essay, I will explore the advantages of flexible schedules and outline alternative methods for motivation.
Providing flexible working arrangements is a highly effective method to ensure employee loyalty and reduce staff turnover. This is primarily because individuals seek a healthy work-life balance to spend more time with their families, and controlling their own schedules helps achieve this goal. Consequently, organizations can boost overall morale and deter workers from seeking employment elsewhere. For instance, allowing remote work on certain days is a primary incentive that motivates staff to remain committed. Conversely, firms that demand strict office attendance may lose talented professionals.
Although flexibility is recognized as a major factor in employee satisfaction, it can sometimes disrupt team communication, potentially hindering company operations. Employers can utilize different approaches to inspire their workforce, such as offering professional development courses or creating a more comfortable office environment, which can significantly improve output. For example, many tech companies provide free meals and recreational areas to ensure their staff feel valued during long working hours.
In conclusion, while flexible working conditions can successfully lower employee turnover, it is not always feasible for every type of business to adopt this model. Nevertheless, other workplace enhancements can be implemented to foster a similarly productive environment.

Giving employees control over their own schedules definitely boosts morale. But it's hardly the only way to build a happy, productive team. While flexible hours have clear benefits, businesses have plenty of other tools at their disposal to keep people motivated.
When companies let people set their own hours or work from home, they usually see a huge drop in staff turnover. Most of us are just trying to juggle our careers with our personal lives, and if a job makes that balancing act easier, we're much more likely to stick around. We're also more likely to ignore recruiters. Just look at the rise of hybrid work—giving someone the freedom to skip the morning commute twice a week builds an incredible amount of loyalty. On the flip side, rigid companies that demand five days a week at a desk often watch their best talent walk out the door.
That said, flexibility isn't a silver bullet. In some industries, scattered schedules can completely derail team communication and slow down projects. When that's the case, managers need to get creative. Investing in a team's future through training programs, or simply making the office a place people actually want to be, often does the trick. You see this a lot in the tech world, where catered lunches and comfortable breakout spaces are used to keep stress low during crunch time.
Ultimately, letting people work on their own terms is a fantastic way to keep them around, but it just doesn't work for every business model. Luckily, companies that require people on-site still have plenty of ways to make their teams feel genuinely valued.

"Work is not everything!" is one of the most famous sayings while talking about careers. It is argued that topics around work, promotions, and professional success are becoming dramatically more frequent between humans in different countries around the world. In my opinion, the extreme part of it is a negative development; although, it has some positivities. I will address some of both sides in this essay.
Talking about work during the major part of the day might make the person look like a workaholic and this is an attitude that majority of the community would not like, thus, might effect on the person's social life. Moreover, having the same topic in mind all day might lead to an obsession, and as a result, this could lead to a depression because the person might not find the proper way to reach to the professional position that meets their satisfaction, which could lead to depression, or even worse such as going through unethical paths where it could destroy their careers entirely!
Nevertheless, the person should be aware of his /her skills and career path and try to enhance their professional position to the best possible way. Also, the competition that we are currently living and the noticeable decrease in job security could be a driver for people to talk about work more often.
In conclusion, people should not be obsessed in work and to talk with everyone about it all the time. However, I believe that people should discuss this matter in order to benefit from each other, and to find more ways to enhance their quality of life.

"Work isn't everything" is a common refrain, yet conversations about career advancement, promotions, and the daily hustle seem to be dominating our interactions more than ever. While there are certainly benefits to being career-minded, I believe that obsessing over professional success does significantly more harm than good.
If someone constantly talks about their job, they quickly come across as one-dimensional. This hyper-focused mindset can easily alienate friends and damage a person's social life. Furthermore, fixating entirely on climbing the corporate ladder often breeds intense anxiety. When people fail to reach their own lofty expectations, the disappointment can trigger deep depression. In extreme cases, the desperation to succeed might even push individuals toward unethical, career-ending choices just to get ahead.
That being said, completely ignoring your professional trajectory isn't wise either. It makes sense to periodically assess your skills and look for ways to grow in your field. Given today's hyper-competitive job market and widespread economic uncertainty, it is entirely understandable why people are so focused on securing their livelihoods and discussing their next career moves.
Ultimately, while letting career ambitions consume your every waking thought is unhealthy, having open conversations about work can actually be quite constructive. Sharing advice, networking, and discussing goals can help people navigate their industries and build more stable, fulfilling lives.

Today smartphones are used almost everywhere, it is impossible to imagine our life without social media, GPS, mobile applications and other digital services. It is reasonable to think that people look forward to the future of mobile technology. Smartphones make our life easier, we can easily communicate with anyone or get directions to any place we plan to visit in a second using a mobile device. Scientists predict that in the nearest future it will be possible to project realistic holograms using our phones and control all household appliances with our minds like we see in science fiction movies. According to forecasts of tech experts, advanced applications will replace jobs of bank tellers, and travel agents and personal assistants. Some companies in South Korea are already selling digital robot companions, which help lonely people to keep their schedules organized.
Despite the fact that smartphones help us, they make us dependent. Apparently, people spend more time looking at screens than ever before. And some of them feel a need for more time to be spent with people in live contact. In addition, a breakdown of one of the important networks of a specific provider can entail serious consequences. To mention the server problem that occurred in the early 2020s, a problem related to a global internet outage and economic losses that were predicted. Fortunately permanent disasters did not happen. However, it is difficult to imagine what could happen if all the networks crashed completely.
We live in a connected era, smartphones penetrated everywhere with all benefits they provide and all dangers they hide. However we are satisfied with them and sometimes we even thank them because they help us in communicating, studying, doing business, entertaining and saving lives in critical situations.

It’s hard to imagine getting through a single day without a smartphone. These devices have completely embedded themselves into our routines, giving us instant access to social media, navigation, and an endless stream of apps. Given how much they simplify our lives—whether that means instantly pulling up directions to a new restaurant or video chatting with someone across the globe—it’s no wonder people are excited to see where mobile technology goes next. 
Looking ahead, tech developers envision a future where our phones integrate seamlessly with augmented reality, letting us project 3D holograms right in our living rooms. We are also seeing a massive shift in how daily services operate. Advanced AI applications are steadily phasing out traditional roles like bank tellers and travel agents. In places like South Korea, tech companies are even rolling out AI-driven digital companions designed to help people manage their daily schedules and combat loneliness.
But this extreme convenience comes at a cost: we are heavily dependent on our screens. Screen time is at an all-time high, leaving many people craving genuine, face-to-face interaction. Beyond the social impact, our reliance on these digital networks creates massive vulnerabilities. When a major server goes down—like the widespread global internet outages we've seen in recent years—the economic and logistical disruptions are immediate. It is unsettling to think about the sheer chaos a permanent network failure would cause.
Ultimately, we live in an era defined by connectivity. While our devices carry real risks and keep us tethered to screens, it is hard to deny their value. They keep us in touch, drive our businesses, entertain us, and can even be lifesavers during an emergency.

Nowadays, voice messages are used in a comparatively more frequent and regular way instead of traditional phone calls. I assume that it happens because of the need of making daily communication more convenient and multitasking. Without the necessity of typing long texts or arranging a live call, it is easier to share your thoughts on the go. This option is highly demanded in a modern temp of life. This essay will cover both advantages and disadvantages of this development.
The increasing use of audio messages provides some pros. First of all, without typing, the process of sending a message requires less amount of effort. Then the sender can just speak naturally and explain the discussed issue much faster. Moreover, this way of communication supports emotional expression between members of a dialogue. Then it is easier to understand the mood of the interlocutor and feel a deeper connection during the conversation.
On the other hand, the development of relying on voice messages has some cons. Firstly, communication becomes less convenient for the receiver. Then the listener has almost no opportunity to hear the message in public places without headphones. Text messages are significant in situations where silence is required. Secondly, sometimes the main gist of the audio goes lost, because spoken language is less structured. In these conditions, the main point of the recording can be hidden among unnecessary pauses or filler words. If participants of the communication are in a hurry, they will not get the information quickly.
In conclusion I would like to say that the process of using voice messages in a more frequent way has both advantages and disadvantages. It can be beneficial or annoying in different situations. That is why everyone should have their own opinion based on the information discussed in this essay.

Voice notes have quickly become a go-to alternative to traditional phone calls and long text messages. This shift makes perfect sense given how much we value multitasking today; leaving a quick audio clip lets people share their thoughts on the fly without having to stop and type or schedule a live conversation. While this trend certainly fits our fast-paced lifestyles, relying heavily on voice messages comes with both distinct benefits and a few noticeable drawbacks.
On the plus side, recording a memo requires practically zero effort compared to typing out a lengthy paragraph. You can just talk naturally, which usually means you can explain a complex situation much faster. Beyond just saving time, audio captures tone of voice and emotion in a way that plain text simply cannot. Hearing someone's actual voice helps convey their mood, making the exchange feel much more personal and connected than a standard chat thread.
However, the convenience for the sender often becomes a burden for the receiver. Unlike a text that can be glanced at anywhere, a voice message demands a quiet environment or a pair of headphones. In meetings or noisy public spaces, listening to an audio clip is basically impossible. Furthermore, people tend to ramble when they speak. A point that could have been a concise sentence often gets buried under pauses and tangents. If the listener is in a rush, digging through a two-minute recording to find one specific detail is incredibly frustrating.
Ultimately, swapping texts for voice messages is a double-edged sword. Whether it is a helpful tool or a minor annoyance really depends on the context of the conversation and the environment of the people involved.

Elsewhere, Africa and Asia both enjoyed steady increases in their export capacity. African shipments grew by 15 million tons overall, while Asian output actually more than doubled by the end of 2015. Central America followed a slightly different pattern; although local producers successfully ramped up exports for the first decade, they faced a minor setback at the very end of the period, with volumes dropping by roughly 8 million tons.

Studying with a group or as a team is a great way of gaining more academic knowledge, however, there are study methods which can be done alone that will also build some important study habits. Both ones are important in achieving academic success. Below, we will see which one of them is a great way of gaining more important academic skills.
First of all, we need to understand that studying in a group, or as a team can help us deepen our understanding, because they provide us with the ability to share notes and learn from each other which is something crucial in our school lives as students as we were all enrolled to learn together in classrooms. In addition, group or team studying will not only allow us to know the other classmates sitting next to us but to figure out what their perspectives are and to be helpful to them when they need us. However, studying alone has also its own benefits.
In fact, personal study sessions or some of the assignments we do alone can give us some understanding about how the academic material is structured as well. Firsthand, sometimes we need to do a few readings alone as this can help us to develop some personal important study skills to know exactly what we really struggle with and what is our academic goal for the semester. Second, doing personal study habits such as reading quietly, or reviewing flashcards will not only strengthen our memory but also to be mentally focused.
In conclusion, both of them have positive and negative aspects to consider. However, in my opinion, studying in a group or as a team can teach more important academic skills than those study sessions which are done alone because group studying lets us explore the concepts around us which will later teach us important knowledge which is necessary for our graduation.

While group study sessions are fantastic for expanding your academic knowledge, hitting the books on your own is just as crucial for developing essential study habits. Both approaches play a major role in a student's success. It is worth exploring, however, which method actually provides the most long-term value for a student's educational growth.
Collaborating with peers is incredibly helpful for deepening our understanding of a complex subject. When we study in teams, we get the chance to share notes, compare viewpoints, and fill in each other's knowledge gaps. Since schools are naturally communal environments, learning to work alongside classmates is a vital part of the experience. Beyond just passing tests, group work teaches us how to listen to different perspectives and support our peers when they struggle with a difficult concept.
That being said, solo studying brings its own unique advantages. Working through assignments independently forces us to grapple with the material directly. Sometimes, we just need to sit alone with a textbook to figure out exactly which topics we find challenging and how to tackle them without relying on someone else's input. Furthermore, solitary habits like quiet reading or reviewing flashcards are fantastic for building memory retention and improving our mental focus over long stretches of time.
Ultimately, both study methods have clear benefits depending on the situation. I tend to believe, though, that collaborative studying is the superior way to build essential skills. By exploring concepts together, students develop a much richer, multifaceted understanding of the material—something that is absolutely critical not just for graduation, but for thriving in the real world.

Most factories release a massive amount of toxic waste into local water sources. This, in turn, has grown into an environmental issue of global concern that governments across the world believe can be mitigated by adding heavy taxes on industrial corporations. I chiefly disagree with the effectiveness of this method as next to nothing has been altered since the policy enforcement due to economic issues.
The number of companies utilizing cheap disposal methods has not dwindled. Even though many eco-friendly alternatives had been suggested, there was no affordable infrastructure for the use of average manufacturing plants. Mid-sized businesses do not possess sufficient income to shift to environmentally friendly solutions for the fear of financial hardships. For instance, a green filtration system would necessitate a massive installation space inside a factory that not many possess. As a consequence, the more convenient and profitable solution to pay the tax and continue polluting is resorted to.
The population of the world as a whole demands more products. As more and more people are born, there is a growing need for goods to be manufactured in a short span of time. Although a few corporations may be able to abandon their old methods in exchange for sustainable practices, a significant portion of the growing industry still opts for paying environmental fines to get products out faster.
In conclusion, while the idea of the tax policy introduction bore few fruits, they had not been enough to make a contribution to solving industrial pollution. Ironically, corporations would rather overspend on taxes for their convenience than think about nature.

Industrial pollution is one of the most pressing environmental crises we face today, and many governments have tried to fix it by slapping heavy taxes on factories that dump waste. While the intention is good, I honestly don't think this approach works. In reality, very little has actually changed since these financial penalties were introduced, mostly because it remains easier for companies to simply pay the fees and carry on.
Despite the new taxes, factories haven't stopped relying on cheap, harmful disposal methods. We constantly hear about green alternatives, but the infrastructure just isn't there for the average manufacturing plant to make the switch affordably. Smaller and mid-sized businesses simply don't have the profit margins to overhaul their entire operation without risking bankruptcy. Upgrading to a high-tech water filtration system, for example, requires an enormous amount of physical space and operational downtime that most facilities can't spare. Because of this, managers usually decide that taking the financial hit from a pollution tax is the safer, more practical business choice.
Furthermore, global demand for cheap consumer goods is higher than ever. With a rapidly growing population, factories are under immense pressure to churn out products at breakneck speeds. While a handful of high-profile brands might be able to pivot to completely sustainable manufacturing, the vast majority of the industrial sector doesn't have that luxury. They prefer to pay the fines and keep their assembly lines moving without interruption.
Ultimately, while taxing industrial waste might have seemed like a strong deterrent on paper, it hasn't actually put a dent in global pollution levels. It turns out that when pushed, most corporations are more than willing to eat the cost of a fine if it means protecting their bottom line and maintaining their convenience.

Some people claim that spatial navigation skills are still beneficial in spite of the appearance of applications providing GPS navigation in real time. Modern software is able to give quite accurate directions to different places, and it seems that it will soon replace physical maps and will get rid of the necessity to learn geography. However, in my opinion, there are still some essential advantages to knowing how to navigate independently.
First of all, the physical environment is such a complicated and constantly changing system that it is impossible to automate the navigation process to keep all details. For instance, if a road is closed due to sudden construction, it will unlikely be recognized by GPS immediately. Thus, it is too hard to teach a device to understand all the local changes that you might encounter.
Another important aspect of learning navigation is knowing your surroundings. Spatial awareness is a great source for understanding the layout of a city and its neighborhoods. If you use online maps, you will never know the actual geography or landmarks that contain historical value.
Moreover, learning spatial navigation is a tool of training your brain. It helps to develop memory and spatial intelligence. For instance, people didn't refuse studying math after the calculation device had been invented, because it is still important to understand how it works and it is necessary for developing other sciences.
Finally, there is a lot of places and occasions when you do not have a battery charge or internet access. Then digital maps are not available. But if you need to find your way being in a remote area, it is better to know at least some basic orientation skills. Otherwise, you can occur in an unpleasant situation.
To sum up, I definitely agree with the statement that learning navigation skills is essential in spite of technology progress. Although GPS is able to direct you anywhere you want, it is crucial to continue learning to navigate in order to understand your surroundings, to know your city and understand its peculiarities and, of course, to make your brain work.

Even with GPS and real-time navigation apps on every smartphone, many argue that basic map-reading and spatial skills are still incredibly valuable. It is easy to assume that digital maps will completely erase our need to understand geography, given how accurately they provide turn-by-turn directions. Still, I believe relying entirely on a screen is a mistake, and knowing how to navigate on your own offers some major advantages. 
For one thing, the physical world is messy and unpredictable. A GPS app might not immediately register a sudden road closure, a flooded street, or a newly built detour. If you lack a basic mental map of an area, you are completely helpless the moment the software fails to adapt to real-world conditions. Beyond just avoiding traffic, navigating by yourself actually connects you to your environment. Constantly staring at a blue dot on a screen means you miss out on the actual layout of a city. Learning to orient yourself helps you understand how neighborhoods connect and allows you to discover landmarks you would otherwise just drive past.
Building spatial awareness is also a fantastic way to keep your mind sharp. Forcing yourself to remember routes and visualize directions actively develops your memory. Think about it like basic math: we didn't stop teaching addition and subtraction just because calculators were invented. Understanding the underlying logic of where you are is still crucial.
There is also the obvious issue of technology simply failing. Batteries die, and remote areas frequently lack cell service. If you are hiking or driving through the mountains and lose your signal, knowing how to read a physical map or orient yourself isn't just an outdated hobby—it can keep you out of a genuinely dangerous situation.
Ultimately, while navigation apps are undeniably convenient, they shouldn't entirely replace our natural sense of direction. Developing spatial awareness keeps our brains active, helps us truly experience the places we travel, and ensures we are never left stranded just because a phone died.

The past twenty years have seen a dramatic change in the number of employees who work from home. In this essay, I will explore the advantages and disadvantages of this trend.
On the one hand, both employees and their companies can benefit from this trend. First, workers can choose more comfortable environments for themselves because there are more flexible options. The office settings and daily routines are quite different. Secondly, people can save their commuting time, they can stay with their family when they are in their own house, and they quickly improve their work-life balance. Thirdly, it helps to promote the productivity and efficiency of the business. These employees manage their schedules independently. Workers can get better focus on their tasks. Managers in the company will get more completed projects without office distractions. Similarly, the businesses also can get reduced costs regarding office space.
On the other hand, there are disadvantages too. Firstly, it costs people a lot of money to set up a home office. Many individuals have to spend their savings to buy computers and equipment to support their work at home. Secondly, some workers cannot stand the big isolation from their colleagues, they lose their motivation and quit their jobs.
To conclude, given the fact that working from home helps employees with their daily routines and promotes the productivity of companies, even if there are some drawbacks, I think the benefits outweigh the problems.

Over the last couple of decades, the shift toward remote work has completely transformed the traditional nine-to-five routine. While ditching the daily commute sounds like a dream to many, working from home comes with its own unique set of pros and cons that are worth exploring.
There are definitely clear benefits for both staff and employers. For one thing, people finally have the freedom to design a workspace that actually suits them, rather than being stuck in a rigid corporate cubicle. Without the stress of rush hour traffic, employees gain hours back every single week. This extra time at home naturally leads to a much healthier work-life balance. Beyond personal well-being, businesses often see a massive boost in overall productivity. When people can manage their own schedules and block out office distractions, they tend to get a lot more done. Plus, companies can save a fortune on commercial real estate when half their workforce is logging in from their living rooms.
That being said, the remote lifestyle isn't perfect. Setting up a proper home office isn't cheap, and many workers find themselves spending a lot of money out of pocket just to buy reliable Wi-Fi routers, ergonomic chairs, and extra monitors. Furthermore, not everyone handles isolation well. Being cut off from the casual, day-to-day banter with coworkers can leave some people feeling completely disconnected, which sometimes leads to burnout or even quitting altogether.
Ultimately, while working remotely requires a solid initial setup and can occasionally get lonely, I believe the advantages far outweigh the downsides. The flexibility it provides makes for happier employees and more efficient businesses.

In the diagram, the steps needed to obtain fresh pasta dough are illustrated, and figures are provided for the measurements and time required for the procedure.
In the initial stage, the main ingredients, namely, 2 cups of semolina flour, 3 large eggs, and a half teaspoon of olive oil respectively, are combined together in a bowl.
After being transferred to a wooden board, the initial mixture undergoes through 2 kneading stages, with the first at a gentle pace for 5 minutes, and the second at a vigorous pace for 10 minutes.
Subsequently, the dough is going to be left to rest wrapped in plastic film to allow the gluten to relax, which it would take at least 30 minutes.
Having rolled out the dough by pressing it through a machine, the resulting sheet of pasta is either cut thoroughly into thin strands equally sized or making the entire piece into wide ribbons for pappardelle.

The provided diagram breaks down the process of making fresh pasta dough, including the required ingredient measurements and estimated preparation times.
To start, you combine the base ingredients in a large bowl: two cups of semolina flour, three whole eggs, and about half a teaspoon of olive oil. Once the ingredients are fully mixed, the dough is moved to a wooden board for kneading. This hands-on process happens in two distinct phases. First, you knead the mixture gently for five minutes to bring everything together. After that, you work the dough much more vigorously for another ten minutes until it becomes smooth and elastic.
Next, the dough needs some time to rest. It is wrapped tightly in plastic film and left to sit for at least half an hour, which gives the gluten enough time to relax. Finally, the rested dough is flattened into a thin sheet using a pasta machine. From there, you can either slice the sheet into thin, evenly sized strands for spaghetti, or cut the whole piece into much wider ribbons to make pappardelle.

The diagram shows the process of tea from production to preparing for sale in supermarkets and shops.
Overall, it is certain that tea goes through 10 different stages from picking the leaves to packing in boxes.
Firstly, tea leaves are picked and then withered by humans. The withered leaves are later rolled in a machine then in the oxidation room, it's fermented slowly. Furthermore, after fermenting they go through a firing process where they turned into dry flakes, then sorted with high-tech equipment after a while, it goes from grading process.
We are now arriving at the later process where tea is now blended in the factory. In the third last stage, again it goes from the sorting process but this time it is flavored and after this, they put them in a packaging machine where it weighs the portions of tea to make them precise.
Lastly, the tea is ready to pack in the box and ready to sell on the market.

The provided diagram illustrates the step-by-step process of producing tea, from the initial harvesting of the leaves to the final retail packaging. Overall, the production involves ten distinct stages, beginning with hand-picking and ending with the finished product boxed up for consumers.
The process begins when fresh tea leaves are manually harvested and laid out to wither. Once the leaves have lost a sufficient amount of moisture, they are fed into a rolling machine. This prepares them for the oxidation room, where they are left to slowly ferment. Following fermentation, the leaves undergo a high-heat firing process that completely dries them out, turning them into crisp flakes. Afterward, specialized equipment is used to sort and grade the tea based on size and quality.
In the final stages of production, the graded leaves are blended. Depending on the specific product line, they may also be flavored at this point. Next, the tea is routed into an automated packaging machine that precisely weighs out the required portions. Finally, the processed tea is sealed into boxes and distributed to supermarkets for sale.

The line graph and chart below show data on the average daily maximum and minimum temperatures in degrees Celsius and the average number of days with rainfall each month, from January to December, for Sydney and Melbourne in Australia.
Overall, both cities have similar tendencies throughout the entire period. Likewise, Melbourne has lower temperatures than Sydney during the whole year. In addition, Sydney has a higher average number of days with rainfall than Melbourne during most of the year.
To start with, Melbourne's maximum average daily temperatures have a significant decrease in tendency, from 26 degrees in January to only 13 degrees in July. In contrast, Sydney's daily maximum temperatures present lower fluctuations from 27 degrees in January to just 18 degrees in July. Additionally, Sydney reaches its highest maximum average daily temperature in February at 28 degrees, whilst Melbourne reaches its lowest minimum average daily temperature in July and August at 6 degrees. Furthermore, the average number of days with rainfall decreases moderately during the spring season in Sydney, from 15 days in March to 8 days in September, October and November. However, this is the opposite tendency in the average number of days with rainfall in Melbourne which slightly increases from 8 days in February to 14 days in July, August and September.
To sum up, Melbourne has rainy and cool winters, whilst Sydney has rainy and warm autumns.

The provided charts compare the average monthly temperatures and rainfall frequency for Sydney and Melbourne over the course of a year. Overall, while both cities follow the same general seasonal cycle, Melbourne stays noticeably cooler than Sydney year-round. Sydney also tends to experience more rainy days, though the two cities see their wettest weather at completely different times of the year.
Looking at the temperature data, Melbourne's climate is much more variable. Its average daily maximum drops sharply from a summer peak of 26 degrees in January down to just 13 degrees by July. Sydney, on the other hand, enjoys much milder seasonal shifts. Its warmest days average around 28 degrees in February, and the city only cools down to about 18 degrees mid-year. During the dead of winter—specifically July and August—Melbourne's overnight lows routinely dip to a chilly 6 degrees.
Rainfall trends also move in opposite directions. In Sydney, rainy days are most frequent in early autumn, peaking at about 15 days in March before drying out to around 8 days per month throughout the spring. Melbourne sees the reverse pattern. Its late summers are relatively dry, with just 8 rainy days in February, but the city gets steadily wetter as the year progresses, averaging 14 rainy days a month from July through September.
Ultimately, Melbourne is characterized by its cold, damp winters, whereas Sydney sees its heaviest rainfall while the weather is still warm.

It is not uncommon for teenagers to follow social media influencers. From daily vloggers to fashion models, many young people around the world look up to these internet personalities and attempt to emulate their lifestyles. In my opinion, allowing children to idolize influencers can often be detrimental, as they frequently set unrealistic expectations.
Many of today’s social media stars tend to present heavily edited versions of reality, and they promote standards that are unattainable for young people. For instance, some popular creators use digital filters to showcase flawless appearances and luxurious vacations. In addition, the internet often rewards influencers who engage in dramatic controversies or dangerous pranks to gain views. These types of content can have a negative influence on impressionable teens who might falsely believe that they should act similarly to gain attention or social validation.
Unrealistic lifestyles aren’t the only issue, as many internet personalities are also well-known for their relentless promotion of consumerism. They routinely advertise expensive beauty products or trendy fashion items without any consideration for the financial burden it places on their audience. This kind of behaviour is dangerous because it is normalizing hyper-consumerism and teaching teens that purchasing advertised goods will bring them happiness instead of focusing on what really matters in life: genuine experiences, personal growth, and hard work.
To conclude, young adults should keep in mind that the internet's perception of success and beauty is based on curated, superficial things rather than what actually matters. In the grand scheme of things, it would be far more beneficial for teenagers to spend time developing their own self-esteem rooted in real-world achievements and authentic friendships.

It seems like almost every teenager today follows at least a few social media influencers. Whether they are watching daily vloggers or fashion icons, kids everywhere are glued to these online personalities, often trying to copy how they dress and live. But honestly, I think treating influencers like role models does significantly more harm than good, primarily because they are selling a reality that simply doesn't exist.
The main issue is that these internet stars curate heavily edited versions of their lives, pushing lifestyle standards that are entirely out of reach for the average kid. A quick scroll through a feed usually turns up endless photos tweaked with digital filters, showing off flawless skin and non-stop, expensive vacations. On top of that, social media platforms tend to reward creators who pull dangerous stunts or stir up petty drama just to get clicks. For an impressionable teenager, consuming this content daily can be incredibly damaging. They can easily fall into the trap of thinking they need to act out or look physically perfect just to be liked or noticed by their peers.
Beyond the fake lifestyles, there is also the relentless pressure to buy things. Influencers are practically walking billboards, constantly pushing their followers to purchase the latest skincare routine or fast-fashion haul without caring if these items are actually affordable for their young audience. This endless cycle of sponsored posts is dangerous. It normalizes hyper-consumerism and convinces kids that buying the right products is the fast track to happiness, pulling their attention away from things that actually matter like personal growth and real-world experiences.
Ultimately, teenagers need to understand that the glossy success they see online is highly curated and entirely superficial. Rather than trying to emulate a stranger's digital highlight reel, kids would be much better off focusing on their actual lives—building authentic friendships, working hard, and developing genuine self-confidence.

This bar chart illustrates how global energy consumption is distributed among four major nations across three distinct years: 1980, 2010, and a projected outlook for 2040. Traveling plays an essential role in broadening the perspective of an individual. Some people believe that trips which are taken in groups are more beneficial like guided tours, while others think that solo travel like backpacking alone is better. The following essay will discuss both arguments and give my opinion that trips which are taken in groups have more advantages over solo travel activities.
First of all, there is no doubt that individual travel activities enhance people's independence. It helps building the problem-solving skills and strengthen the adaptability of the individuals. For example, one famous travel blogger in my home country was exploring Europe alone, he mentioned once in an interview, that traveling helped him not only to see landmarks but it raised his self-reliance and shaped his personality.
However, some people believe that, trips which are taken in groups have more advantages and all members will get the benefits. Group tours, for example, will help the travelers specifically the young adults to understand compromising together and sharing the experiences. In addition people will grow with better understanding to the concept of sharing memories which can improve all the relationships. For instance, I was a member of a travel club in university, me and my friends were taught, in order to enjoy trips we should put itineraries together and work as one unit. Furthermore, it helps me later in my life to understand the meaning of collaboration and how the sharing planning and traveling together could bring the success to our journey.
To conclude, solo travel can bring independence and self-discovery to individuals but trips which taken in groups teach people the meaning of experiencing the world together which will improve the interpersonal skills in the future.

Traveling is one of the best ways to broaden your perspective. When it comes to planning a trip, people often debate whether it is better to join a group tour or head out backpacking entirely alone. While flying solo definitely has its perks, I firmly believe that exploring with others offers a much richer and more rewarding experience.
There is no denying that traveling alone forces you to become more independent. Navigating a foreign city by yourself builds incredible problem-solving skills and makes you highly adaptable. I remember reading an interview with a well-known travel blogger who spent a year exploring Europe on his own; he credited that solo journey with completely transforming his self-reliance and shaping who he became as an adult.
Despite those benefits, sharing a journey with a group brings unique advantages that you simply cannot get on your own. For younger travelers in particular, group trips teach the invaluable art of compromise. You learn how to navigate different personalities and share responsibilities, whether that means splitting the cost of accommodation or taking turns picking out restaurants. I actually joined a university travel club a few years ago, and organizing our itineraries taught us how to operate as a cohesive team. Looking back, that experience really improved my ability to collaborate. It showed me that working together to overcome inevitable travel hiccups usually leads to a much more successful and memorable trip.
Ultimately, while exploring the world by yourself is a great way to discover your own independence, traveling alongside a group fosters deep connections and teaches vital interpersonal skills that last a lifetime.

The bar graphs shed light on impetuses driving people to choose the major of Data Science and job applicants' qualities considered by tech recruiters.
Overall, there are mainly two features manifested by the diagrams. First, high salary expectations are the major reason for choosing this degree. Second, coding proficiency and relevant internship experience are regarded as the most critical qualities by a majority of employers.
According to the first chart, the percentage of interviewees deeming high salaries to be the major reason for choosing this major reaches 60%, while any other reason is reported by less than 25%. Notably, contributing to academic research is the most uncommon driving force, reported by only 5%.
As shown in the second chart, the percentage of recruiters valuing coding proficiency most is the same as that of employers attaching the greatest importance to internship experience, each reaching 30%. Communication skills and problem-solving abilities are reported by 15% of employers respectively. Formal certifications are deemed to be the essential quality by the smallest percentage of recruiters, 10%.

These two bar charts break down why students decide to pursue a degree in Data Science, alongside what tech recruiters actually prioritize when making hiring decisions. Right away, two main trends stand out: students are heavily motivated by the prospect of a high salary, whereas employers are looking for hard coding skills and real-world experience.
When it comes to choosing the major, the promise of a lucrative paycheck clearly dominates. A full 60% of the surveyed students pointed to high salary expectations as their primary motivation, leaving all other reasons trailing behind at under 25%. Interestingly, a desire to contribute to academic research barely registered as a driving force, motivating just 5% of the group.
The employer data reveals exactly what it takes to actually land a job in the field. Hiring managers placed the highest premium on practical capability, with coding proficiency and previous internship experience tying for the top spot at 30% each. Soft skills, such as communication and general problem-solving, were less of a priority, capturing 15% of the vote respectively. Perhaps surprisingly, formal certifications ranked at the very bottom of the list, considered an essential quality by only 10% of the recruiters surveyed.

Nowadays, social media has become without doubt the most popular, engaging and accessible form of entertainment and communication. It has dramatically changed how teenagers interact with each other and how they form their identities. With only one push of a button on their devices like a smartphone or a tablet, they can share their daily activities with friends who are thousands of miles away or watch entertaining videos without a need for going outside. However, this convenience of social media carries some potential threats to young users such as negative impacts on their mental health and exposure to inappropriate content. Therefore, the public should demand some forms of age regulations on social media to reduce these risks.
Certainly, the use of social media needs to be regulated if we want to preserve the mental well-being of young people. Unfortunately, there is an enormous pressure to maintain a perfect image on the Internet. For example, teenagers eagerly consume a lot of content about unrealistic beauty standards, luxurious lifestyles, and filtered photos. However, the algorithms of these applications very often promote this content to target them and influence their self-esteem. This practice should be controlled and we as a society need to have a say how digital content should be recommended to minors on the Internet. For this reason, we need to demand stricter algorithms to have better control how media affects young minds on social platforms.
Another risk of having social media free from regulation is that we have no means of protection from harmful trends. I find this risk very dangerous since many children have used these platforms as their primary source of entertainment and rely on them to spend their free time. Now, anybody can upload a video as long as he or she has access to the Internet. Simply, people can create and post their media that often don’t have any age-appropriate filters. We have witnessed this danger during the viral spread of dangerous online challenges. Many kids who participated in these challenges made up their mind watching some videos on TikTok or Instagram that depicted risky behavior. These videos are allowed to be posted, because the tech companies don’t have any strict obligation to verify the age of their users. Clearly, this wouldn’t happen if there were some forms of regulations that can verify better the age of users on the Internet, so the young viewers can avoid the risk of harmful exposure.
Surely, social media can no longer stay without any regulatory laws. These policies are necessary to protect youth mental health and keep them safe from dangerous trends. Simply, the regulation policies of social networks give parents more power to be in control when their children use these platforms to interact or consume media.

There is no denying that social media completely dominates how we connect and entertain ourselves today. It has fundamentally rewired how teenagers build relationships and figure out who they are. With just a quick swipe on a phone, kids can chat with distant friends or scroll through endless streams of videos instead of hanging out in person. But giving children unrestricted access to these platforms comes with massive downsides—namely, serious mental health struggles and exposure to wildly inappropriate content. Because of this, it is becoming clear that we need much stricter age regulations across these apps.
If we actually want to protect the mental well-being of the younger generation, we have to start policing how these platforms operate. Right now, the pressure to look and act perfect online is suffocating. Teenagers are constantly bombarded with heavily edited photos, impossible beauty standards, and influencers flaunting luxury lifestyles. Worse still, the algorithms are designed to push this exact type of content directly into their feeds to keep them hooked, often destroying their self-esteem in the process. We shouldn't just accept this as normal. We desperately need oversight to dictate how these recommendation algorithms target minors, giving us a fighting chance to protect their mental health.
Beyond the psychological toll, a lack of age verification leaves kids completely exposed to harmful trends. Many children now spend the bulk of their free time scrolling, treating social media as their main source of entertainment. The problem is that anyone with an internet connection can upload anything, usually without dealing with any meaningful content filters. Just look at the wave of dangerous viral challenges we've seen in recent years. Kids end up attempting incredibly risky stunts simply because they watched a few trending videos on their feeds. Tech companies currently get away with hosting this material because they aren't legally forced to verify how old their users actually are. If strict age gates were mandated by law, we could finally stop young, impressionable users from stumbling into this kind of danger.
Ultimately, the "Wild West" era of social media has to end. Implementing firm age restrictions isn't about censorship; it's a necessary step to safeguard youth mental health and block access to dangerous trends. Putting these laws in place would finally give parents the leverage they need to keep their kids safe online.

The two pie charts depict the outcome of a survey about the customer service of the Riverside Restaurant in 2010 and 2015 based on 100 diners.
Overall, 5 scales of customer satisfaction are illustrated on each pie chart. In both years, the bulk of customer feedback was generally positive and beneficial to the restaurant. However, in 2010, the two highest rated opinions were relatively small, but they became the most dominant in 2015.
In 2010, guests provided a notable amount of negative reviews, ranging between satisfactory and very poor. Nevertheless, this trend updated to a highly positive one, ranging between excellent and good in the year 2015. In five years, even though the worst feedback experienced almost a fourfold decrease, the poor review category dropped significantly as well in 2015.
Regarding the feeling of positive customer service, there was an approximate 30% growth for both outstanding and good restaurant service combined. It is also notable that the medium satisfaction level experienced a downward trend over the five years, decreasing from 40% to 15%.

These two pie charts break down the results of customer satisfaction surveys conducted at the Riverside Restaurant in 2010 and 2015, capturing the feedback of 100 diners each year. Right away, you can see a massive improvement in how people viewed their dining experience over that five-year span.
Back in 2010, the restaurant struggled with a noticeable chunk of negative feedback. A significant portion of the respondents rated their visits anywhere from just "satisfactory" down to "very poor." By 2015, however, the situation had completely turned around. The absolute worst reviews plummeted to a quarter of what they used to be, and the standard "poor" ratings shrank dramatically alongside them.
As those negative scores faded, the higher ratings naturally surged. The combined share of diners who felt the service was "excellent" or "good" jumped by roughly 30%. Interestingly, as more people moved into these top-tier categories, the middle-of-the-road "satisfactory" rating took a steep dive, dropping from 40% of the total responses in 2010 down to just 15% in 2015. Ultimately, the data shows that the restaurant clearly managed to elevate its average, everyday service into a genuinely outstanding experience.
